title: Math-Booklet
text:
Welcome to MYP 5 Mathematics
Welcome to your comprehensive revision hub. This isn’t just a textbook; it’s a living document designed to evolve with you as we move toward your final MYP 5 assessments.

title: Math-Booklet
text:
How to use this Booklet



🎯 Mastery Tracking
Every unit starts with clear Learning Targets. Use these as your personal checklist. If you can’t tick it off, you aren’t ready for the test!


🗂️ Formula Cards
No more hunting through notebooks. Quick-access digital cards provide the identities and rules you need at a glance.




⚡ Dynamic Practice
Our “Test Your Knowledge” sections feature a “Try Again” button that generates new numbers for the same problem type. Practice the method, not the memory.


🔍 Instant Feedback
Every exercise includes a hidden solution. Toggle it only after you’ve put pen to paper.

title: Unit 1: Operations
text: 🎯 Learning Targets

title: Unit 1: Operations
text:
1


Calculate with Integers – Add, subtract, multiply, and divide positive and negative numbers accurately.




2


Apply Order of Operations – Evaluate numerical expressions using PEMDAS/BODMAS, including brackets and exponents.




3


Operate with Fractions – Add, subtract, multiply, and divide fractions and mixed numbers fluently.




4


Work with Decimals – Perform calculations involving decimals and convert between fractions, decimals, and percentages.




5


Simplify Complex Calculations – Combine integers, fractions, and decimals in multi-step numerical problems.

title: Unit 1: Operations
text:
🗂️ Recall: Formula Card

Essential Operation Rules

Simplifying Fractions

💡 Pro Tip A fraction is fully simplified when GCF = 1


How to Simplify

Find the Greatest Common Factor (GCF) of numerator and denominator
Divide both top and bottom by the GCF

\[\frac{a}{b} = \frac{a \div \text{GCF}}{b \div \text{GCF}}\]
Examples: - \(\frac{12}{18} = \frac{12 \div 6}{18 \div 6} = \frac{2}{3}\) - \(\frac{15}{25} = \frac{15 \div 5}{25 \div 5} = \frac{3}{5}\)



Rules of Signs (Multiplication & Division)

🎯 Memory Trick Same signs = Happy (+)
Different signs = Sad (-)


Same Signs → Positive - \((+) \times (+) = (+)\) - \((-) \times (-) = (+)\)
Different Signs → Negative - \((+) \times (-) = (-)\) - \((-) \times (+) = (-)\)



Adding/Subtracting Fractions

⚠️ Common Mistake Don’t add denominators!
WRONG: \(\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{3} = \frac{2}{5}\)


Same Denominator: Keep denominator, add/subtract numerators \[\frac{a}{c} + \frac{b}{c} = \frac{a + b}{c}\]



Multiplying & Dividing Fractions

📝 Quick Method Cancel BEFORE you multiply - saves time!


Multiply: \(\frac{a}{b} \times \frac{c}{d} = \frac{a \times c}{b \times d}\)
Divide (Keep-Change-Flip): \(\frac{a}{b} \div \frac{c}{d} = \frac{a}{b} \times \frac{d}{c}\)



Order of Operations (PEMDAS/BODMAS)


Parentheses / Brackets
Exponents / Orders
M/D Multiplication & Division (Left to Right)
A/S Addition & Subtraction (Left to Right)

Calculate

\(\frac{1}{3}\) of 24

Tip🔍 Solutions

Topic: Fractions of Amounts Skills: Multiplicative Reasoning


∑ × π ÷ √ ◆ ∞ ± ∫ ≠ Δ







ImportantLevel 2: Applied Context



Calculate:
a) Zaid earns 800 QAR a month. He spends \(\frac{1}{4}\) on rent and \(\frac{2}{5}\) on food. How much money does he have left?
b) Fatima is buying shoes that normally cost 200 QAR. They are \(\frac{1}{5}\) off. What is the sale price?
c) Amir has 300 marbles. He gives \(\frac{1}{2}\) to his brother and \(\frac{1}{3}\) of the remaining marbles to his friend. How many does he keep?








Tip🔍 Solutions






Rent: \((800 \div 4) \times 1 = 200\)
Food: \((800 \div 5) \times 2 = 320\)
Total spent: \(200 + 320 = 520\)
Left: \(800 - 520 = 280\) QAR
Discount: \((200 \div 5) \times 1 = 40\)
Sale Price: \(200 - 40 = 160\) QAR
To brother: \(300 \times \frac{1}{2} = 150\)
Remaining: \(150\)
To friend: \(150 \times \frac{1}{3} = 50\)
Kept: \(150 - 50 = 100\)





Topic: Fractions of Amounts
Skills: Word Problems




Exercise 1.3 (Time and Fractions) A train is late. It should arrive at 5 pm but arrives at 5:15 pm.

How many minutes late is the train?

Write your answer as a fraction of an hour.







Tip🔍 View Solution






15 minutes b) \(\frac{15}{60} = \mathbf{\frac{1}{4}}\) of an hour





Topic: Time and Fractions Skills: Fraction Simplification



∑ × π ÷ √ ◆ ∞ ± ∫ ≠ Δ


Exercise 1.4 (Savings and Values) For every £200 Mrs. Joy earns, she saves £34.

Work out £34 as a percentage of £200.

Last month she earned £1000. How much does she save?







Tip🔍 View Solution






\(\frac{34}{200} \times 100 = \mathbf{17\%}\)

\(17\%\) of \(£1000 = 0.17 \times 1000 = \mathbf{£170}\)





Topic: Savings Skills: Values as Percentages



∑ × π ÷ √ ◆ ∞ ± ∫ ≠ Δ









ImportantMulti-Step Reasoning




Maryam works 8 hours a week and earns 50 QAR per hour. She decides to save \(\frac{2}{5}\) of her total weekly earnings. How many weeks will it take her to save 640 QAR?
There are 40 students in a class. \(\frac{3}{5}\) are girls. \(\frac{1}{4}\) of the girls wear glasses. How many girls in the class wear glasses?









Tip🔍 Solutions






Weekly Pay: \(8 \times 50 = 400\)
Weekly Savings: \((400 \div 5) \times 2 = 160\)
Weeks needed: \(640 \div 160 = 4\) weeks
Total Girls: \((40 \div 5) \times 3 = 24\)
Girls with glasses: \((24 \div 4) \times 1 = 6\)





Topic: Fractions of Amounts
Skills: Logic & Savings

title: 2  Unit 2: Indices
text: 2.1 🎯 Learning Targets

title: 2  Unit 2: Indices
text:
1


Apply Multiplication Rules – Simplify expressions by adding powers when multiplying terms with the same base.




2


Apply Division Rules – Simplify expressions by subtracting powers when dividing terms with the same base.




3


Master Power of a Power – Use the rule to simplify expressions where a power is raised to another power.




4


Manage Coefficients – Accurately multiply or divide numerical coefficients separately from the algebraic index laws.




5


Handle Negative Indices – Correctly perform addition and subtraction of exponents when calculations involve negative numbers.




6


Simplify Complex Fractions – Combine multiple index laws to simplify multi-step algebraic fractions involving multiplication and division.

title: 2  Unit 2: Indices
text:
2.2 🗂️ Formula Card

Algebraic Indices & Expansion Rules

Laws of Indices (The Core Rules)

💡 Pro Tip These rules ONLY apply when the base (the big letter) is the same.


Multiplication & Division 1. Multiplication: Add the powers \(\rightarrow\) \[x^a \times x^b = x^{a+b}\] 2. Division: Subtract the powers \(\rightarrow\) \[\frac{x^a}{x^b} = x^{a-b}\]
Examples: \[y^5 \times y^3 = y^{5+3} = y^8\] \[10w^7 \div 2w^3 = (10 \div 2)w^{7-3} = 5w^4\]



Power of a Power & Zero

🎯 Memory Trick Brackets mean multiply!
Anything to the power of \(0\) is a hero (it becomes \(1\)).


The Bracket Rule: \[(x^a)^b = x^{a \times b} = x^{ab}\]
Examples: \[(k^3)^4 = k^{3 \times 4} = k^{12}\] \[(5x^2)^3 = 5^3 \times x^{2 \times 3} = 125x^6\]
The Zero Rule: \[x^0 = 1\]



Negative & Fractional Indices

⚠️ Common Mistake A negative power does NOT make the number negative; it makes it a reciprocal (fraction)!


Negative Powers: \[x^{-a} = \frac{1}{x^a}\]
Fractional Powers: \[x^{\frac{1}{n}} = \sqrt[n]{x}\]
Examples: \[4^{-2} = \frac{1}{4^2} = \frac{1}{16}\] \[9^{\frac{1}{2}} = \sqrt{9} = 3\]



Expanding Brackets with Indices

📝 Quick Method Multiply the “Big” numbers (coefficients), add the “Small” numbers (indices).


Single Brackets: \[a(b + c) = ab + ac\]
Examples: \[3x^2(2x^3 + 4) = (3 \times 2)x^{2+3} + (3 \times 4)x^2 = 6x^5 + 12x^2\] \[x^5(x^2 - x^{-3}) = x^{5+2} - x^{5-3} = x^7 - x^2\]



Algebraic Fractions with Indices

Simplifying Fractions:

Simplify the numerator (top) first using multiplication laws.
Simplify the denominator (bottom).
Use the division law (subtract) to find the final result.

Example: \[\frac{a^5 \times a^4}{a^2} = \frac{a^{5+4}}{a^2} = \frac{a^9}{a^2} = a^{9-2} = a^7\]
Example with Coefficients: \[\frac{(2x^3)^2}{4x^4} = \frac{4x^6}{4x^4} = 1x^{6-4} = x^2\]

title: 3  Unit 3: Mensuration
text: 3.1 🎯 Learning Targets

title: 3  Unit 3: Mensuration
text:
🔵 Volume



1


Volume — Prisms
Calculate the volume of right prisms using
\(V = A_{\text{base}} \times l\)
Includes rectangular, triangular, trapezoidal, and parallelogram cross-sections.




2


Volume — Cylinders
Calculate the volume of a full cylinder using
\(V = \pi r^2 h\)
Use either radius or diameter.




3


Volume — Portions
Find volumes of partial cylinders:
- Half: \(\tfrac{1}{2}\pi r^2 h\)
- Quarter
- Sector: \(\tfrac{\theta}{360}\pi r^2 h\)




4


Volume — Reverse
Rearrange volume formulas to find unknown dimensions such as length, height, or radius.




5


Volume — Context
Solve real-world problems involving volume, including converting m³ to litres.




6


Composite Volume
Break complex solids into simpler shapes and add or subtract their volumes.






🟢 Surface Area



7


SA — Prisms
Calculate surface area of cuboids, triangular and trapezoidal prisms, and open boxes.




8


SA — Cylinders
Calculate surface area of: - Closed cylinders
- Open-top cylinders
- Tubes and half-cylinders




9


SA — Composite
Find surface area of composite solids, including added or removed faces.






🟣 Advanced & Algebra



10


Unit Conversions
Convert between length, area, and volume units using correct powers of conversion.




11


Pyramids, Cones & Spheres
- Pyramid: \(V=\tfrac{1}{3}Ah\)
- Cone: \(V=\tfrac{1}{3}\pi r^2h\), \(SA=\pi r^2+\pi rl\)
- Sphere: \(V=\tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3\), \(SA=4\pi r^2\)
Includes composite solids.




12


Algebraic Volume
Work with algebraic dimensions:
- Expand and simplify expressions
- Solve for \(x\) given volume

title: 3  Unit 3: Mensuration
text:
3.2 🗂️ Formula Cards

Unit 3: Mensuration — Formula Reference


📏 Unit Conversions

💡 Key Rule When converting units:
• Length → multiply or divide by the factor
• Area → use the factor squared
• Volume → use the factor cubed


Length chain: \(\text{km} \xrightarrow{\times 1000} \text{m} \xrightarrow{\times 100} \text{cm} \xrightarrow{\times 10} \text{mm}\)
Area chain: \(\text{km}^2 \xrightarrow{\times 10^6} \text{m}^2 \xrightarrow{\times 10^4} \text{cm}^2 \xrightarrow{\times 100} \text{mm}^2\)
Volume chain: \(\text{km}^3 \xrightarrow{\times 10^9} \text{m}^3 \xrightarrow{\times 10^6} \text{cm}^3 \xrightarrow{\times 10^3} \text{mm}^3\)
Capacity: \(1\text{ m}^3 = 1000\text{ L}\) \(\qquad\) \(1\text{ cm}^3 = 1\text{ mL}\)


⚠️ Common Error \(1\text{ m}^2 = 100\text{ cm}^2\) ✗ — should be 10 000 cm².
\(1\text{ m}^3 = 100\text{ cm}^3\) ✗ — should be 1 000 000 cm³.




📦 Volume Formulae

🎯 Core Idea For ANY prism or cylinder:
\[V = A_{\text{cross-section}} \times \text{length}\]










Solid
Cross-section
Formula




Rectangular prism
Rectangle
\(V = l \times w \times h\)


Triangular prism
Triangle
\(V = \tfrac{1}{2}\,b\,h\,l\)


Trapezoidal prism
Trapezoid
\(V = \tfrac{1}{2}(a+b)\,h\,l\)


Cylinder
Circle
\(V = \pi r^2 h\)


Half-cylinder
Semicircle
\(V = \tfrac{1}{2}\pi r^2 h\)


Quarter-cylinder
Quarter circle
\(V = \tfrac{1}{4}\pi r^2 h\)


Cylindrical sector
Sector (\(\theta°\))
\(V = \dfrac{\theta}{360}\,\pi r^2 h\)



Composite solids: \(V_{\text{total}} = V_1 + V_2\) (add) or \(V_1 - V_2\) (subtract for holes)


⚠️ Common Errors
- Using diameter instead of radius: \(r = d \div 2\) first!
- Missing the \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) for triangular cross-sections.
- Adding volumes when you should subtract (hollow solids).




🎨 Surface Area Formulae

📝 Strategy Surface Area = sum of areas of ALL outer faces.
Sketch the solid, label every face, calculate each, then add.


Cuboid (all 6 faces): \[SA = 2(lw + lh + wh)\]
Open-top box (5 faces — no lid): \[SA = lw + 2lh + 2wh\]
Triangular prism (5 faces): \[SA = 2 \times A_{\triangle} + (b + s_1 + s_2) \times l\]
Trapezoidal prism (6 faces): \[SA = 2 \times \tfrac{1}{2}(a+b)h + (a + b + s_1 + s_2) \times l\]
Closed cylinder (curved + 2 circles): \[SA = 2\pi rh + 2\pi r^2\]
Open-top cylinder (curved + 1 circle): \[SA = 2\pi rh + \pi r^2\]
Tube/pipe (curved only, both ends open): \[SA = 2\pi rh\]
Half-cylinder (3 regions): \[SA = \underbrace{\pi r h}_{\text{half-curved}} + \underbrace{2rh}_{\text{flat rect}} + \underbrace{\pi r^2}_{\text{2 semicircles}}\]


💡 Cylinder Unrolled The curved surface of a cylinder unrolls into a rectangle:
width \(= 2\pi r\) (the circumference), height \(= h\).
So curved \(SA = 2\pi r \times h = 2\pi rh\).


⚠️ Common Errors — Surface Area
- Using the full cylinder SA formula when the cylinder is open at one or both ends.
- Forgetting to include both triangular ends of a triangular prism.
- Counting an internal join face that is not on the outside surface.
- Confusing SA (cm²) with Volume (cm³).




🔺 Pyramids, Cones and Spheres

🎯 Key Pattern Pyramids and cones are exactly ⅓ the volume of the prism or cylinder with the same base and height.










Solid
Volume
Surface Area




Pyramid (any base)
\(V = \dfrac{1}{3} A_{\text{base}} \times h\)
sum of all faces


Rectangular pyramid
\(V = \dfrac{1}{3} lwh\)



Square pyramid
\(V = \dfrac{1}{3} s^2 h\)



Cone
\(V = \dfrac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h\)
\(SA = \pi r^2 + \pi r l\)


Sphere
\(V = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3\)
\(SA = 4\pi r^2\)


Hemisphere
\(V = \dfrac{2}{3}\pi r^3\)
\(SA = 3\pi r^2\)



Cone slant height: \(l = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2}\) (Pythagoras — slant is the hypotenuse)
Remember: \(r = d \div 2\) always before substituting.


⚠️ Common Errors — Curved Solids
- Forgetting the \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) in pyramid and cone formulas.
- Squaring \(r\) when you should cube it (sphere formula uses \(r^3\)).
- Using \(\tfrac{4}{3}\) instead of \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) for hemisphere volume.
- Using the perpendicular height \(h\) instead of slant \(l\) in cone SA.




🔣 Algebraic Volume

📝 Method Substitute the algebraic expressions into the volume formula, then expand brackets and collect like terms using index laws.


Steps:

Write the formula: e.g. \(V = \tfrac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times h \times l\)
Substitute each expression: e.g. base \(= 2x\), \(h = x\), \(l = (x+4)\)
Multiply step by step: \(V = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 2x \times x \times (x+4) = x^2(x+4)\)
Expand: \(V = x^3 + 4x^2\)

Index law reminder: \((ax)^2 = a^2x^2\) — square both the coefficient AND the variable.
Example — solve for x: If \(V = 3x^3 = 375\), then \(x^3 = 125\), so \(x = \sqrt[3]{125} = 5\).


⚠️ Common Errors — Algebraic Volume
- \((2x)^2 = 4x^2\) ✓ not \(2x^2\) ✗
- Stopping before expanding all brackets.
- Forgetting \(V =\) on the left-hand side of the formula.
- Square rooting when the equation requires a cube root.

title: 3  Unit 3: Mensuration
text:
3.8 🔣 Part F — Algebraic Volume






Note📌 What to expect



These questions give dimensions as algebraic expressions (e.g. \(2x\), \(x+3\)). Apply the volume formula, expand brackets, and simplify using index laws.



🔴 Challenging



Exercise 3.54 (Algebraic Volume — Rectangular Prism) Express V in terms of x.

\[\text{Rect prism: }l=4x,\ w=3x,\ h=7\text{ cm.}\ \text{Express }V.\]
\[\text{Rect prism: }l=3x,\ w=3x,\ h=4\text{ cm.}\ \text{Express }V.\]
\[\text{Rect prism: }l=5x,\ w=3x,\ h=5\text{ cm.}\ \text{Express }V.\]
\[\text{Rect prism: }l=2x,\ w=2x,\ h=8\text{ cm.}\ \text{Express }V.\]
\[\text{Rect prism: }l=3x,\ w=2x,\ h=6\text{ cm.}\ \text{Express }V.\]
\[\text{Rect prism: }l=2x,\ w=2x,\ h=8\text{ cm.}\ \text{Express }V.\]







Tip🔍 View Solution






\[V=84x^2\,\text{cm}^{3}\]
\[V=36x^2\,\text{cm}^{3}\]
\[V=75x^2\,\text{cm}^{3}\]
\[V=32x^2\,\text{cm}^{3}\]
\[V=36x^2\,\text{cm}^{3}\]
\[V=32x^2\,\text{cm}^{3}\]






Algebraic expressions Index laws

\[\text{Tri-prism: base}=2x,\ h=x,\ \text{length}=(x+8)\text{ cm.}\ \text{Find }V.\]

\[\text{Tri-prism: base}=2x,\ h=x,\ \text{length}=(x+3)\text{ cm.}\ \text{Find }V.\]







Tip🔍 View Solution






\[V=x^3+8x^2\]
\[V=x^3+2x^2\]
\[V=x^3+5x^2\]

title: 4  Unit 4: Statistics
text:
4.2 🗂️ Formula Cards

Statistics — Key Formulas & Rules

Averages and Range

💡 Pro Tip Always sort your data first before finding the median or quartiles.


Mean: \[\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x}{n} = \frac{\text{sum of all values}}{\text{number of values}}\]
Median: Middle value when data is ordered.
If \(n\) is even: mean of the two middle values.
Mode: Most frequently occurring value.
Range: \[\text{Range} = \text{maximum} - \text{minimum}\]
Example: Data: \(3, 7, 7, 9, 14\) \[\bar{x} = \frac{3+7+7+9+14}{5} = 8 \qquad \text{Median} = 7 \qquad \text{Mode} = 7 \qquad \text{Range} = 11\]



Mean from a Frequency Table

🎯 Memory Trick Multiply each value by its frequency first — then divide by the total frequency, not the number of rows!


\[\bar{x} = \frac{\sum fx}{\sum f}\]



\(x\)
\(f\)
\(fx\)




2
3
6


4
7
28


6
5
30


Total
15
64



\[\bar{x} = \frac{64}{15} \approx 4.3\]



Grouped Frequency Tables

⚠️ Common Mistake Use the midpoint of each class — not the lower or upper boundary!


Estimated mean: \[\bar{x} \approx \frac{\sum fx}{\sum f} \quad \text{where } x = \text{midpoint of class}\]
Modal class: Class with the highest frequency.
Median class: Class containing the \(\dfrac{n}{2}\)th value (use cumulative frequency).
Example: Class \(10 \leq x < 20\), frequency \(= 8\) → midpoint \(x = 15\), so \(fx = 120\)



Cumulative Frequency

📝 Quick Method Read across from the CF axis to the curve, then down to the \(x\)-axis.


Building CF table: Each row = previous cumulative total \(+\) current frequency.
Reading quartiles from a CF graph (total = \(n\)):



Measure
Read at CF value




Lower quartile \(Q_1\)
\(\dfrac{n}{4}\)


Median \(Q_2\)
\(\dfrac{n}{2}\)


Upper quartile \(Q_3\)
\(\dfrac{3n}{4}\)



\[\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1\]



Box Plots and Outliers

Five-number summary: Min, \(Q_1\), Median, \(Q_3\), Max
Box plot: Box spans \(Q_1\) to \(Q_3\). Median line inside. Whiskers extend to min and max.
Outlier rule: \[\text{Outlier if: } x > Q_3 + 1.5 \times \text{IQR} \quad \text{or} \quad x < Q_1 - 1.5 \times \text{IQR}\]
Example: \(Q_1 = 35,\ Q_3 = 55,\ \text{IQR} = 20\)
Upper fence \(= 55 + 1.5 \times 20 = 85\)
Any value \(> 85\) is an outlier.



Combined Mean

💡 Pro Tip You cannot just average two means — you must use the totals!


\[\bar{x}_{\text{combined}} = \frac{n_1 \bar{x}_1 + n_2 \bar{x}_2}{n_1 + n_2}\]
Example: Group 1: \(n=100\), mean \(= 18.2\). Group 2: \(n=50\), mean \(= 17.3\) \[\bar{x} = \frac{100 \times 18.2 + 50 \times 17.3}{150} = \frac{1820 + 865}{150} = \frac{2685}{150} = 17.9\]

Exercise 4.6 (Find Missing Value / Updated Mean) Find the missing value or new mean.



\[\text{Mean} = 14,\quad n=6\\[4pt]\text{Numbers: }14,\ 8,\ 13,\ 7,\ 4,\ x\]



\[\text{Mean} = 26,\quad\text{three values: }30,\ 35,\ 30\\[4pt]\text{Other two in ratio }1:4\]



\[\text{Mean} = 11,\quad n=5\\[4pt]\text{Numbers: }15,\ 14,\ 8,\ 4,\ x\]



\[\text{Mean of }6\text{ numbers} = 3,\quad\text{new value} = 12\]






Tip🔍 View Solution





a) \[x = 38\]
\[\quad \text{Total} = 14\times6=84\quad x = 84-(14+8+13+7+4) = 38\]
b) \[7\text{ and }28\]
\[\quad \text{Step 1: Total}=26\times5=130\\[4pt]\text{Step 2: Remaining}=130-(30+35+30)=35\\[4pt]\text{Step 3: Ratio }1:4\text{ → }5\text{ equal parts, each }=\frac{35}{5}=7\\[4pt]\text{Step 4: Numbers are }7\text{ and }28\]
c) \[x = 14\]
\[\quad \text{Total} = 11\times5=55\quad x = 55-(15+14+8+4) = 14\]
d) \[\text{New mean} = 4.29\]
\[\quad \text{Old total} = 3\times6 = 18\quad \text{New total} = 18+12 = 30\quad \bar{x}_{\text{new}} = \frac{30}{7} = 4.29\]

Exercise 4.19 (Combined Mean (Two Groups)) Find the combined mean of the two groups.



\[n_1=50,\ \bar{x}_1=17.8\\[4pt]n_2=50,\ \bar{x}_2=10.1\]



\[n_1=120,\ \bar{x}_1=23.1\\[4pt]n_2=50,\ \bar{x}_2=13.7\]



\[n_1=80,\ \bar{x}_1=23.3\\[4pt]n_2=20,\ \bar{x}_2=15.5\]



\[n_1=50,\ \bar{x}_1=23.9\\[4pt]n_2=50,\ \bar{x}_2=21.7\]






Tip🔍 View Solution





a) \[\text{Combined mean} = 13.95\]
\[\quad \frac{50\times17.8+50\times10.1}{50+50}=\frac{890.0+505.0}{100}=\frac{1395.0}{100}=13.95\]
b) \[\text{Combined mean} = 20.34\]
\[\quad \frac{120\times23.1+50\times13.7}{120+50}=\frac{2772.0+685.0}{170}=\frac{3457.0}{170}=20.34\]
c) \[\text{Combined mean} = 21.74\]
\[\quad \frac{80\times23.3+20\times15.5}{80+20}=\frac{1864.0+310.0}{100}=\frac{2174.0}{100}=21.74\]
d) \[\text{Combined mean} = 22.8\]
\[\quad \frac{50\times23.9+50\times21.7}{50+50}=\frac{1195.0+1085.0}{100}=\frac{2280.0}{100}=22.8\]





Weighted mean Total from mean




Exercise 4.20 (Find Missing Frequencies (Grouped)) Find the values of a and b.



\[n = 100,\quad \bar{x} \approx 43.5\\[6pt]{\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.6}\begin{array}{lc}
\hline \text{Class} & \text{Frequency} \\[4pt]
\hline 20 \leq x < 30 & 15 \\[4pt]

\hline 40 \leq x < 50 & 35 \\[4pt]
\hline 50 \leq x < 60 & a \\[4pt]
\hline 60 \leq x < 70 & b \\[4pt]
\hline \end{array}}\]



\[n = 100,\quad \bar{x} \approx 43.5\\[6pt]{\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.6}\begin{array}{lc}
\hline \text{Class} & \text{Frequency} \\[4pt]
\hline 20 \leq x < 30 & 15 \\[4pt]

\hline 40 \leq x < 50 & 35 \\[4pt]
\hline 50 \leq x < 60 & a \\[4pt]
\hline 60 \leq x < 70 & b \\[4pt]
\hline \end{array}}\]



\[n = 100,\quad \bar{x} \approx 43.5\\[6pt]{\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.6}\begin{array}{lc}
\hline \text{Class} & \text{Frequency} \\[4pt]
\hline 20 \leq x < 30 & 15 \\[4pt]
\hline 30 \leq x < 40 & 25 \\[4pt]
\hline 40 \leq x < 50 & 35 \\[4pt]
\hline 50 \leq x < 60 & a \\[4pt]
\hline 60 \leq x < 70 & b \\[4pt]
\hline \end{array}}\]



\[n = 150,\quad \bar{x} \approx 48\\[6pt]{\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.6}\begin{array}{lc}
\hline \text{Class} & \text{Frequency} \\[4pt]
\hline 20 \leq x < 30 & 13 \\[4pt]
\hline 30 \leq x < 40 & 15 \\[4pt]
\hline 40 \leq x < 50 & 40 \\[4pt]
\hline 50 \leq x < 60 & a \\[4pt]
\hline 60 \leq x < 70 & b \\[4pt]
\hline \end{array}}\]






Tip🔍 View Solution





a) \[a = 10,\ b = 15\]
\[\quad \text{Step 1: }a+b=100-75=25\\[4pt]\text{Step 2: }\Sigma fx=43.5\times100=4350\\[4pt]\text{Known }\Sigma fx=2825\quad\Rightarrow 55a+65b=1525\\[4pt]\text{Step 3: Solve simultaneously: }a=10,\ b=15\]
b) \[a = 10,\ b = 15\]
\[\quad \text{Step 1: }a+b=100-75=25\\[4pt]\text{Step 2: }\Sigma fx=43.5\times100=4350\\[4pt]\text{Known }\Sigma fx=2825\quad\Rightarrow 55a+65b=1525\\[4pt]\text{Step 3: Solve simultaneously: }a=10,\ b=15\]
c) \[a = 10,\ b = 15\]
\[\quad \text{Step 1: }a+b=100-75=25\\[4pt]\text{Step 2: }\Sigma fx=43.5\times100=4350\\[4pt]\text{Known }\Sigma fx=2825\quad\Rightarrow 55a+65b=1525\\[4pt]\text{Step 3: Solve simultaneously: }a=10,\ b=15\]
d) \[a = 78,\ b = 4\]
\[\quad \text{Step 1: }a+b=150-68=82\\[4pt]\text{Step 2: }\Sigma fx=48\times150=7200\\[4pt]\text{Known }\Sigma fx=2650\quad\Rightarrow 55a+65b=4550\\[4pt]\text{Step 3: Solve simultaneously: }a=78,\ b=4\]

4.7 🎯 Part E — Box Plots and IQR






Note🔑 Key Points



\[\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1\]
Outlier rule: A value is an outlier if it is \(> Q_3 + 1.5 \times \text{IQR}\) or \(< Q_1 - 1.5 \times \text{IQR}\)



🟢 Easy🟡 Medium🔴 Challenging

Exercise 4.31 (Identify Outliers (1.5 × IQR Rule)) Identify outliers using the 1.5 × IQR rule and draw a box plot.

20406080100MinQ₁Q₂Q₃MaxBox Plot with Outlier







Tip🔍 View Solution





a) \[Q_1=24,\ Q_3=65,\ \text{IQR}=41\\\text{Upper fence}=126.5\\107>126.5\Rightarrow\text{outlier}\]
\[\quad \text{Step 1: }Q_1=24,\ Q_3=65\\[4pt]\text{Step 2: IQR}=Q_3-Q_1=65-24=41\\[4pt]\text{Step 3: Upper fence}=Q_3+1.5\times\text{IQR}=65+1.5\times41=126.5\\[4pt]\text{Step 4: }107>126.5\therefore\text{ outlier }\checkmark\]

title: 5  Unit 5: Linear Equations

#
I can…
Key Skill




LT1
Solve one-step equations using inverse operations
Addition, subtraction, multiplication, division


LT2
Solve two-step equations, including brackets and fractions
Two inverse operations in sequence


LT3
Solve equations with unknowns on both sides
Collecting like terms


LT4

Single and double brackets

LT6

Number riddles, ages, geometry, formulas

title: 5  Unit 5: Linear Equations
text:
5.2 🗂️ Formula Card

Linear Equations — Solving Strategies

The Balance Method

💡 Golden Rule Whatever you do to one side, you must do to the other side.


One-step \[x + 5 = 12 \;\Rightarrow\; x = 12 - 5 = 7\] \[3x = 21 \;\Rightarrow\; x = 21 \div 3 = 7\]
Two-step — undo addition/subtraction first, then multiplication/division \[2x + 3 = 11 \;\Rightarrow\; 2x = 8 \;\Rightarrow\; x = 4\]



Unknowns on Both Sides

🎯 Strategy Move all \(x\) terms to one side, all numbers to the other.


Method: \[5x + 2 = 2x + 8\] \[5x - 2x = 8 - 2\] \[3x = 6 \;\Rightarrow\; x = 2\]



Expanding Brackets

⚠️ Remember The number outside multiplies every term inside the bracket.


Single bracket: \[3(2x + 5) = 27 \;\Rightarrow\; 6x + 15 = 27 \;\Rightarrow\; x = 2\]
Two brackets: \[2(x + 3) + 4(x - 1) = 18\] \[2x + 6 + 4x - 4 = 18\] \[6x + 2 = 18 \;\Rightarrow\; x = \dfrac{8}{3}\]



Algebraic Fractions

📝 Key Move Multiply every term by the denominator (or LCM) to clear fractions first.


Single fraction: \[\dfrac{2x + 1}{3} = 5 \;\Rightarrow\; 2x + 1 = 15 \;\Rightarrow\; x = 7\]
Fractions on both sides — cross multiply: \[\dfrac{x + 1}{2} = \dfrac{x + 3}{4} \;\Rightarrow\; 4(x+1) = 2(x+3) \;\Rightarrow\; x = 1\]
Adding fractions — use LCM: \[\dfrac{x}{2} + \dfrac{x}{3} = 5 \;\xrightarrow{\times 6}\; 3x + 2x = 30 \;\Rightarrow\; x = 6\]



Forming Equations from Words

Step-by-step:

Define your variable — let \(x = \ldots\)
Translate each condition into algebra
Form the equation
Solve and check your answer makes sense

Example: Three consecutive integers sum to 51. \[x + (x+1) + (x+2) = 51 \;\Rightarrow\; 3x + 3 = 51 \;\Rightarrow\; x = 16\] Numbers: 16, 17, 18. ✓

e) \(\displaystyle 4(y + 8) = 76\)


f) \(\displaystyle 3(m - 2) = -3\)

\[\begin{align*}
x + 1 = 0 \\ x = -1

\[\begin{align*}
x - 6 = -12 \\ x = -6
\end{align*}\]

Exercise 5.7  


a) \(\displaystyle 7m + 6 = 8m\)


b) \(\displaystyle 5x - 8 = 4x - 6\)




c) \(\displaystyle 10x + 8 = 4x + 2\)


d) \(\displaystyle 3x - 3 = x - 15\)




e) \(\displaystyle 7x + 6 = 2x + 1\)


f) \(\displaystyle 9n + 7 = 4n - 13\)








Tip🔍 View Solutions





a) \(\displaystyle 7m + 6 = 8m\)
\[\begin{align*}
-m + 6 = 0 \\ -m = -6 \\ m = 6
\end{align*}\]
b) \(\displaystyle 5x - 8 = 4x - 6\)
\[\begin{align*}
x - 8 = -6 \\ x = 2
\end{align*}\]
c) \(\displaystyle 10x + 8 = 4x + 2\)
\[\begin{align*}
6x + 8 = 2 \\ 6x = -6 \\ x = -1
\end{align*}\]
d) \(\displaystyle 3x - 3 = x - 15\)
\[\begin{align*}
2x - 3 = -15 \\ 2x = -12 \\ x = -6
\end{align*}\]
e) \(\displaystyle 7x + 6 = 2x + 1\)
\[\begin{align*}
5x + 6 = 1 \\ 5x = -5 \\ x = -1
\end{align*}\]
f) \(\displaystyle 9n + 7 = 4n - 13\)
\[\begin{align*}
5n + 7 = -13 \\ 5n = -20 \\ n = -4
\end{align*}\]

\end{align*}\]

Exercise 5.11  

Sara buys tickets. Adult tickets cost $7 each and child tickets cost $4 each. The total cost is $29. Sara bought 3 adult tickets.
(i) Write a formula for the total cost \(T\).
(ii) How many child tickets were bought?






Tip🔍 View Solution





\[\begin{align*}
\text{(i) } T = 7a + 4c \\ \text{(ii) } 21 + 4c = 29 \\ 4c = 8 \\ c = 2
\end{align*}\]
Answer: \(T = 7a + 4c,\; c = 2\)





Exercise 5.12  

The sum of four consecutive integers is 70.
(i) Let the first integer be \(x\). Write an equation.
(ii) Find the numbers.






Tip🔍 View Solution





\[\begin{align*}
\text{(i) } x + (x + 1) + (x + 2) + (x + 3) = 70 \\ \text{(ii) } 4x + 6 = 70 \\ x = 16 \\ \text{Numbers: } 16, 17, 18, 19
\end{align*}\]
Answer: \(x = 16,\; \text{numbers: } 16, 17, 18, 19\)





Exercise 5.13  

Ibrahim is \(x\) years old. Yusuf is 6 years older than Ibrahim. The sum of their ages is 54.
(i) Form an equation in \(x\).
(ii) Find the ages of Ibrahim and Yusuf.






Tip🔍 View Solution





\[\begin{align*}
\text{(i) } x + (x + 6) = 54 \\ \text{(ii) } 2x + 6 = 54 \\ 2x = 48 \\ x = 24 \\ \text{Ibrahim = 24, Yusuf = 30}
\end{align*}\]
Answer: \(x = 24,\; Ibrahim=24,\; Yusuf=30\)





Exercise 5.14  

The angles of a triangle are \((x + 13)^\circ\), \((2x + 14)^\circ\) and \((3x + 45)^\circ\).

Angles in a triangle sum to \(180^\circ\).

(i) Form an equation in \(x\).
(ii) Find \(x\) and each angle.






Tip🔍 View Solution





\[\begin{align*}
\text{(i) } 6x + 72 = 180 \\ \text{(ii) } x = 18 \\ \text{Angles: }31^\circ,\; 50^\circ,\; 99^\circ
\end{align*}\]
Answer: \(x = 18,\; 31^\circ,\; 50^\circ,\; 99^\circ\)





Exercise 5.15  

The lengths, in cm, of the sides of the triangle are \(3(x + 2)\), \(2x - 2\) and \(2x + 7\).
(i) Write an expression for the perimeter of the triangle.
(ii) The perimeter is 32 cm. Find \(x\).






Tip🔍 View Solution





\[\begin{align*}
\text{(i) } (3(x + 2)) + (2x - 2) + (2x + 7) = 7x + 11 \text{ cm} \\ \text{(ii) } 7x + 11 = 32 \\ 7x = 21 \\ x = 3
\end{align*}\]
Answer: \((i)\; 7x + 11 \text{ cm},\quad (ii)\; x = 3\)





Exercise 5.16  


4(2x + 3)4x - 34x + 1Diagram NOT accurately drawn

The lengths, in cm, of the sides of the triangle are \(4(2x + 3)\), \(4x - 3\) and \(4x + 1\).
(i) Write an expression for the perimeter of the triangle.
(ii) The perimeter is 58 cm. Find \(x\).






Tip🔍 View Solution





\[\begin{align*}
\text{(i) } (4(2x + 3)) + (4x - 3) + (4x + 1) = 16x + 10 \text{ cm} \\ \text{(ii) } 16x + 10 = 58 \\ 16x = 48 \\ x = 3
\end{align*}\]
Answer: \((i)\; 16x + 10 \text{ cm},\quad (ii)\; x = 3\)





Exercise 5.17  


(2x + 16)°(3x + 13)°(3x + 47)°Diagram NOT accurately drawn

The angles of a triangle are \((2x + 16)^\circ\), \((3x + 13)^\circ\) and \((3x + 47)^\circ\).

Angles in a triangle sum to \(180^\circ\).

(i) Form an equation in \(x\).
(ii) Find \(x\) and each angle.






Tip🔍 View Solution





\[\begin{align*}
\text{(i) } 8x + 76 = 180 \\ \text{(ii) } x = 13 \\ \text{Angles: }42^\circ,\; 52^\circ,\; 86^\circ
\end{align*}\]
Answer: \(x=13,\; 42^\circ,\; 52^\circ,\; 86^\circ\)





Exercise 5.18  

The angles of a triangle are \((3x + 22)^\circ\), \((2x + 24)^\circ\) and \((x + 74)^\circ\).

Angles in a triangle sum to \(180^\circ\).

(i) Form an equation in \(x\).
(ii) Find \(x\) and each angle.






Tip🔍 View Solution





\[\begin{align*}
\text{(i) } 6x + 120 = 180 \\ \text{(ii) } x = 10 \\ \text{Angles: }52^\circ,\; 44^\circ,\; 84^\circ
\end{align*}\]
Answer: \(x=10,\; 52^\circ,\; 44^\circ,\; 84^\circ\)





Exercise 5.19  


4x + 6 cmx + 4 cmDiagram NOT accurately drawn

A rectangle has length \((4x + 6)\) cm and width \((x + 4)\) cm.
(i) Write an expression for the perimeter.
(ii) The perimeter is 90 cm. Find \(x\).






Tip🔍 View Solution





\[\begin{align*}
\text{(i) } 2(4x + 6 + x + 4) = 10x + 20 \text{ cm} \\ \text{(ii) } 10x + 20 = 90 \\ 10x = 70 \\ x = 7
\end{align*}\]
Answer: \((i)\; 10x + 20 \text{ cm},\quad (ii)\; x = 7\)





Exercise 5.20  

A rectangle has length \((2x + 8)\) cm and width \((x + 6)\) cm.
(i) Write an expression for the perimeter.
(ii) The perimeter is 64 cm. Find \(x\).






Tip🔍 View Solution





\[\begin{align*}
\text{(i) } 2(2x + 8 + x + 6) = 6x + 28 \text{ cm} \\ \text{(ii) } 6x + 28 = 64 \\ 6x = 36 \\ x = 6
\end{align*}\]
Answer: \((i)\; 6x + 28 \text{ cm},\quad (ii)\; x = 6\)





Exercise 5.21  


2x - 4x + 2x + 5Diagram NOT accurately drawn

An isosceles triangle has equal sides \((2x - 4)\) cm and \((x + 2)\) cm. The base is \((x + 5)\) cm.
(i) Find \(x\).
(ii) Find the perimeter of the triangle.






Tip🔍 View Solution





\[\begin{align*}
\text{(i) } 2x - 4 = x + 2 \\ x = 6 \\ \text{(ii) Perimeter} = 27 \text{ cm}
\end{align*}\]
Answer: \(x = 6,\; P = 27 \text{ cm}\)





Exercise 5.22  

An isosceles triangle has equal sides \((3x + 5)\) cm and \((x + 17)\) cm. The base is \((2x + 2)\) cm.
(i) Find \(x\).
(ii) Find the perimeter of the triangle.






Tip🔍 View Solution





\[\begin{align*}
\text{(i) } 3x + 5 = x + 17 \\ 2x = 12 \\ x = 6 \\ \text{(ii) Perimeter} = 60 \text{ cm}
\end{align*}\]
Answer: \(x = 6,\; P = 60 \text{ cm}\)

Exercise 5.23  


a) \(\displaystyle 2(4x + 2) + 3(x + 2) = -34\)




b) \(\displaystyle 2(y + 4) + 3(4y - 5) = 49\)




c) \(\displaystyle 2(y + 4) + 4(3y - 3) = 66\)




d) \(\displaystyle 4(2t - 2) + 5(2t + 4) = 102\)








Tip🔍 View Solutions





a) \(\displaystyle 2(4x + 2) + 3(x + 2) = -34\)
\[\begin{align*}
8x + 4 + 3x + 6 = -34 \\ 11x + 10 = -34 \\ 11x = -44 \\ x = -4
\end{align*}\]
b) \(\displaystyle 2(y + 4) + 3(4y - 5) = 49\)
\[\begin{align*}
2y + 8 + 12y - 15 = 49 \\ 14y - 7 = 49 \\ 14y = 56 \\ y = 4
\end{align*}\]
c) \(\displaystyle 2(y + 4) + 4(3y - 3) = 66\)
\[\begin{align*}
2y + 8 + 12y - 12 = 66 \\ 14y - 4 = 66 \\ 14y = 70 \\ y = 5
\end{align*}\]
d) \(\displaystyle 4(2t - 2) + 5(2t + 4) = 102\)
\[\begin{align*}
8t - 8 + 10t + 20 = 102 \\ 18t + 12 = 102 \\ 18t = 90 \\ t = 5
\end{align*}\]





Exercise 5.24  


a) \(\displaystyle (n + 3)^{2} = (n + 4)(n + 1)\)


b) \(\displaystyle (3y + 4)^{2} = (3y + 5)(3y + 2)\)




c) \(\displaystyle (2y + 4)^{2} = (2y + 6)(2y + 3)\)


d) \(\displaystyle (2m + 2)^{2} = (2m + 1)(2m + 4)\)








Tip🔍 View Solutions





a) \(\displaystyle (n + 3)^{2} = (n + 4)(n + 1)\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Expand both sides, } 1n^2 \text{ cancels:} \\ 6n + 9 = 5n + 4 \\ 1n = -5 \\ n = -5
\end{align*}\]
b) \(\displaystyle (3y + 4)^{2} = (3y + 5)(3y + 2)\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Expand both sides, } 9y^2 \text{ cancels:} \\ 24y + 16 = 21y + 10 \\ 3y = -6 \\ y = -2
\end{align*}\]
c) \(\displaystyle (2y + 4)^{2} = (2y + 6)(2y + 3)\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Expand both sides, } 4y^2 \text{ cancels:} \\ 16y + 16 = 18y + 18 \\ -2y = 2 \\ y = -1
\end{align*}\]
d) \(\displaystyle (2m + 2)^{2} = (2m + 1)(2m + 4)\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Expand both sides, } 4m^2 \text{ cancels:} \\ 8m + 4 = 10m + 4 \\ -2m = 0 \\ m = 0
\end{align*}\]





Exercise 5.25  


a) \(\displaystyle 5(3y + 2) + 4(3y - 1) = -2(3y + 5)\)




b) \(\displaystyle 5(3p + 1) + 2(3p + 1) = -2(2p + 6)\)




c) \(\displaystyle 4(3y + 1) + 4(y - 2) = -2(y + 5)\)




d) \(\displaystyle 3(2n - 1) + 3(3n - 5) = -1(n - 3)\)








Tip🔍 View Solutions





a) \(\displaystyle 5(3y + 2) + 4(3y - 1) = -2(3y + 5)\)
\[\begin{align*}
15y + 10 + 12y - 4 = -6y - 10 \\ 27y + 6 = -6y - 10 \\ 33y = -16 \\ y = -\dfrac{16}{33}
\end{align*}\]
b) \(\displaystyle 5(3p + 1) + 2(3p + 1) = -2(2p + 6)\)
\[\begin{align*}
15p + 5 + 6p + 2 = -4p - 12 \\ 21p + 7 = -4p - 12 \\ 25p = -19 \\ p = -\dfrac{19}{25}
\end{align*}\]
c) \(\displaystyle 4(3y + 1) + 4(y - 2) = -2(y + 5)\)
\[\begin{align*}
12y + 4 + 4y - 8 = -2y - 10 \\ 16y - 4 = -2y - 10 \\ 18y = -6 \\ y = -\dfrac{1}{3}
\end{align*}\]
d) \(\displaystyle 3(2n - 1) + 3(3n - 5) = -1(n - 3)\)
\[\begin{align*}
6n - 3 + 9n - 15 = -n + 3 \\ 15n - 18 = -n + 3 \\ 16n = 21 \\ n = \dfrac{21}{16}
\end{align*}\]





Exercise 5.26  


a) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{5y + 2}{3} = \dfrac{2y + 26}{4}\)


b) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{4p + 2}{3} = \dfrac{3p + 1}{3}\)




c) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{4n - 2}{2} = \dfrac{n - 4}{4}\)


d) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{3x + 2}{2} = \dfrac{2x}{4}\)








Tip🔍 View Solutions





a) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{5y + 2}{3} = \dfrac{2y + 26}{4}\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Cross multiply: } 4(5y + 2) = 3(2y + 26) \\ 20y + 8 = 6y + 78 \\ 14y = 70 \\ y = 5
\end{align*}\]
b) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{4p + 2}{3} = \dfrac{3p + 1}{3}\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Cross multiply: } 3(4p + 2) = 3(3p + 1) \\ 12p + 6 = 9p + 3 \\ 3p = -3 \\ p = -1
\end{align*}\]
c) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{4n - 2}{2} = \dfrac{n - 4}{4}\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Cross multiply: } 4(4n - 2) = 2(n - 4) \\ 16n - 8 = 2n - 8 \\ 14n = 0 \\ n = 0
\end{align*}\]
d) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{3x + 2}{2} = \dfrac{2x}{4}\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Cross multiply: } 4(3x + 2) = 2(2x) \\ 12x + 8 = 4x \\ 8x = -8 \\ x = -1
\end{align*}\]





Exercise 5.27  


a) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{2p - 1}{2} + \dfrac{2p + 5}{2} = -10\)


b) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{2n + 5}{2} + \dfrac{n - 1}{2} = 17\)




c) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{m - 1}{2} + \dfrac{3m}{5} = -6\)


d) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{p - 1}{5} + \dfrac{2p + 3}{5} = -2\)








Tip🔍 View Solutions





a) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{2p - 1}{2} + \dfrac{2p + 5}{2} = -10\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Multiply by }2: 2p - 1 + 2p + 5 = -20 \\ 4p + 4 = -20 \\ 4p = -24 \\ p = -6
\end{align*}\]
b) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{2n + 5}{2} + \dfrac{n - 1}{2} = 17\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Multiply by }2: 2n + 5 + n - 1 = 34 \\ 3n + 4 = 34 \\ 3n = 30 \\ n = 10
\end{align*}\]
c) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{m - 1}{2} + \dfrac{3m}{5} = -6\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Multiply by }10: 5m - 5 + 6m = -60 \\ 11m - 5 = -60 \\ 11m = -55 \\ m = -5
\end{align*}\]
d) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{p - 1}{5} + \dfrac{2p + 3}{5} = -2\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Multiply by }5: p - 1 + 2p + 3 = -10 \\ 3p + 2 = -10 \\ 3p = -12 \\ p = -4
\end{align*}\]





Exercise 5.28  


a) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{t - 2}{3} - \dfrac{2t + 2}{5} = \dfrac{3t - 1}{3} + \dfrac{t - 19}{3}\)




b) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{2p}{4} - \dfrac{2p - 4}{3} = \dfrac{3p}{2} + \dfrac{p - 32}{3}\)




c) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{y - 2}{4} - \dfrac{3y - 3}{4} = \dfrac{2y - 4}{2} + \dfrac{y + 16}{4}\)








Tip🔍 View Solutions





a) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{t - 2}{3} - \dfrac{2t + 2}{5} = \dfrac{3t - 1}{3} + \dfrac{t - 19}{3}\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Multiply by }15: 5t - 10 - 6t + 6 = 15t - 5 + 5t - 95 \\ -21t = -84 \\ t = 4
\end{align*}\]
b) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{2p}{4} - \dfrac{2p - 4}{3} = \dfrac{3p}{2} + \dfrac{p - 32}{3}\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Multiply by }12: 6p - 8p - 16 = 18p + 4p - 128 \\ -24p = -144 \\ p = 6
\end{align*}\]
c) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{y - 2}{4} - \dfrac{3y - 3}{4} = \dfrac{2y - 4}{2} + \dfrac{y + 16}{4}\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Multiply by }4: y - 2 - 3y - 3 = 4y - 8 + y + 16 \\ -7y = 7 \\ y = -1
\end{align*}\]





Exercise 5.29  


ABCD(6x - 23)°(3x + 59)°(3x + 13)°

ABCD is a trapezium.

BC is parallel to AD.
Angle A = \((6x - 23)°\), Angle B = \((3x + 59)°\), Angle D = \((3x + 13)°\).

(i) Find \(x\).
(ii) Find the size of the largest angle in the trapezium.






Tip🔍 View Solution





\[\begin{align*}
\text{(i) Co-interior: } (6x-23) + (3x+59) = 180 \\ 9x + 36 = 180 \\ x = 16 \\ \text{(ii) } \text{A=}73^\circ,\; \text{B=}107^\circ,\; \text{C=}119^\circ,\; \text{D=}61^\circ \\ \text{Largest = }119^\circ
\end{align*}\]
Answer: \(x=16,\; \text{largest} = 119^\circ\)





Exercise 5.30  


ABCD(5x - 26)°(3x + 54)°(2x + 16)°

ABCD is a trapezium.

BC is parallel to AD.
Angle A = \((5x - 26)°\), Angle B = \((3x + 54)°\), Angle D = \((2x + 16)°\).

(i) Find \(x\).
(ii) Find the size of the largest angle in the trapezium.






Tip🔍 View Solution





\[\begin{align*}
\text{(i) Co-interior: } (5x-26) + (3x+54) = 180 \\ 8x + 28 = 180 \\ x = 19 \\ \text{(ii) } \text{A=}69^\circ,\; \text{B=}111^\circ,\; \text{C=}126^\circ,\; \text{D=}54^\circ \\ \text{Largest = }126^\circ
\end{align*}\]
Answer: \(x=19,\; \text{largest} = 126^\circ\)

title: 6  Unit 6: Quadratic Equations
text: 6.1 🎯 Learning Targets

title: 6  Unit 6: Quadratic Equations
text:
#
Learning Target
I can…




LT1
Factorising — Simple
Factorise \(x^2 + bx + c\) using the sum-product method


LT2
Factorising — Harder
Factorise \(ax^2 + bx + c\) (\(a \neq 1\)) using the \(ac\)-method


LT3
Special Forms
Recognise and factorise difference of two squares and perfect squares


LT4
Solve by Factorising
Solve quadratic equations by factorising


LT5
Quadratic Formula & Discriminant
Apply \(x = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2-4ac}}{2a}\); use \(\Delta = b^2-4ac\) to determine nature of roots and find unknown parameters


LT6
Completing the Square
Write \(ax^2+bx+c\) in completed square form and use it to solve


LT7
Word Problems
Form and solve quadratic equations from real-world contexts (area, integers: consecutive, even, odd)


LT8
Geometry
Apply quadratic equations to geometric problems (right triangles, rectangles, L-shapes, cuboids)


LT9
Roots & Vieta’s Formulas
Form equations from roots; use \(\alpha+\beta = -b/a\) and \(\alpha\beta = c/a\) to find symmetric expressions


LT10
Algebraic Fraction Equations
Solve equations with algebraic fractions by multiplying through to form a quadratic


LT11
Disguised Quadratics
Recognise and solve \(x^4 + bx^2 + c = 0\) and \((x+a)^2 + b(x+a) + c = 0\) using substitution


LT12
Surd Equations
Solve equations involving square roots by squaring both sides; identify and reject extraneous roots


LT13
Indices → Quadratic
Solve exponential equations like \((b^{x+p})^{x+q} = b^k\) by equating exponents to form a quadratic

title: 6  Unit 6: Quadratic Equations
text:
6.2 🗂️ Formula Card

Quadratic Equations — Formula Card

Factorising (a = 1)

💡 Sum-Product Method Find two numbers that multiply to \(c\) and add to \(b\).


Standard form: \(x^2 + bx + c = (x + p)(x + q)\)
where \(p \times q = c\) and \(p + q = b\)
Examples: \[x^2 + 5x + 6 = (x+2)(x+3) \quad [2\times3=6,\;2+3=5]\] \[x^2 - x - 12 = (x+3)(x-4) \quad [3\times(-4)=-12,\;3+(-4)=-1]\]



Factorising (a ≠ 1) — ac Method

🎯 ac Method Multiply \(a \times c\), then find two numbers that multiply to \(ac\) and add to \(b\).


Steps: For \(ax^2 + bx + c\):

Find \(p \times q = ac\) and \(p + q = b\)
Split the middle term: \(ax^2 + px + qx + c\)
Group and factorise

Example: \(2x^2 + 7x + 3\), \(\;ac = 6\), find \(p,q: p\times q=6,\; p+q=7 \Rightarrow 1,6\) \[2x^2 + x + 6x + 3 = x(2x+1) + 3(2x+1) = (x+3)(2x+1)\]



Special Forms

⚠️ Spot These First! Always check for special forms before using other methods.


Difference of Two Squares: \[a^2 - b^2 = (a+b)(a-b)\]
Perfect Square: \[a^2 + 2ab + b^2 = (a+b)^2 \qquad a^2 - 2ab + b^2 = (a-b)^2\]
Examples: \[x^2 - 25 = (x+5)(x-5)\] \[x^2 + 8x + 16 = (x+4)^2\]



Quadratic Formula

📝 Always works! Use when factorising is not possible.


For \(ax^2 + bx + c = 0\): \[x = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}\]
The Discriminant \(\Delta = b^2 - 4ac\):



\(\Delta > 0\)
Two distinct real roots




\(\Delta = 0\)
One repeated root


\(\Delta < 0\)
No real roots



Example: \(x^2 - 5x + 3 = 0\) \[x = \dfrac{5 \pm \sqrt{25-12}}{2} = \dfrac{5 \pm \sqrt{13}}{2}\]



Completing the Square

Monic (\(a = 1\)): \(x^2 + bx + c = \left(x + \dfrac{b}{2}\right)^2 - \left(\dfrac{b}{2}\right)^2 + c\)
Non-monic (\(a \neq 1\)): \(ax^2 + bx + c = a\left(x + \dfrac{b}{2a}\right)^2 - \dfrac{b^2}{4a} + c\)
To solve: Write in form \((x+p)^2 = q\), then \(x = -p \pm \sqrt{q}\)
Example: \(x^2 + 6x + 2 = 0\) \[( x+3)^2 - 9 + 2 = 0 \implies (x+3)^2 = 7 \implies x = -3 \pm \sqrt{7}\]

title: 6  Unit 6: Quadratic Equations
text:
6.3 📝 Practice Problems

🟢 Easy🟡 Medium🔴 Challenging



Exercise 6.1 Factorise each expression.


a) \(\displaystyle n^2 + 3n + 2\)


b) \(\displaystyle y^2 + 8y + 16\)




c) \(\displaystyle x^2 + 11x + 18\)


d) \(\displaystyle x^2 + 17x + 70\)




e) \(\displaystyle y^2 + 3y + 2\)


f) \(\displaystyle x^2 + 13x + 36\)




g) \(\displaystyle m^2 + 13m + 36\)


h) \(\displaystyle n^2 + 12n + 32\)








Tip🔍 View Solutions





a) \(\displaystyle n^2 + 3n + 2\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Find } p, q: p \times q = 2,\; p + q = 3 \\ p = 2,\; q = 1 \\ n^2 + 3n + 2 = (n + 2)(n + 1)
\end{align*}\]
b) \(\displaystyle y^2 + 8y + 16\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Find } p, q: p \times q = 16,\; p + q = 8 \\ p = 4,\; q = 4 \\ y^2 + 8y + 16 = (y + 4)(y + 4)
\end{align*}\]
c) \(\displaystyle x^2 + 11x + 18\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Find } p, q: p \times q = 18,\; p + q = 11 \\ p = 2,\; q = 9 \\ x^2 + 11x + 18 = (x + 2)(x + 9)
\end{align*}\]
d) \(\displaystyle x^2 + 17x + 70\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Find } p, q: p \times q = 70,\; p + q = 17 \\ p = 10,\; q = 7 \\ x^2 + 17x + 70 = (x + 10)(x + 7)
\end{align*}\]
e) \(\displaystyle y^2 + 3y + 2\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Find } p, q: p \times q = 2,\; p + q = 3 \\ p = 1,\; q = 2 \\ y^2 + 3y + 2 = (y + 1)(y + 2)
\end{align*}\]
f) \(\displaystyle x^2 + 13x + 36\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Find } p, q: p \times q = 36,\; p + q = 13 \\ p = 4,\; q = 9 \\ x^2 + 13x + 36 = (x + 4)(x + 9)
\end{align*}\]
g) \(\displaystyle m^2 + 13m + 36\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Find } p, q: p \times q = 36,\; p + q = 13 \\ p = 9,\; q = 4 \\ m^2 + 13m + 36 = (m + 9)(m + 4)
\end{align*}\]
h) \(\displaystyle n^2 + 12n + 32\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Find } p, q: p \times q = 32,\; p + q = 12 \\ p = 4,\; q = 8 \\ n^2 + 12n + 32 = (n + 4)(n + 8)
\end{align*}\]





Exercise 6.2 Factorise each expression.


a) \(\displaystyle m^2 - 2m - 3\)


b) \(\displaystyle y^2 + y - 30\)




c) \(\displaystyle x^2 - 2x - 24\)


d) \(\displaystyle x^2 - 5x - 14\)




e) \(\displaystyle x^2 + 5x - 50\)


f) \(\displaystyle x^2 - x - 72\)




g) \(\displaystyle n^2 + 5n - 14\)


h) \(\displaystyle y^2 + 4y - 60\)








Tip🔍 View Solutions





a) \(\displaystyle m^2 - 2m - 3\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Find } p, q: p \times q = -3,\; p + q = -2 \\ p = 1,\; q = -3 \\ m^2 - 2m - 3 = (m + 1)(m - 3)
\end{align*}\]
b) \(\displaystyle y^2 + y - 30\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Find } p, q: p \times q = -30,\; p + q = 1 \\ p = 6,\; q = -5 \\ y^2 + y - 30 = (y + 6)(y - 5)
\end{align*}\]
c) \(\displaystyle x^2 - 2x - 24\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Find } p, q: p \times q = -24,\; p + q = -2 \\ p = 4,\; q = -6 \\ x^2 - 2x - 24 = (x + 4)(x - 6)
\end{align*}\]
d) \(\displaystyle x^2 - 5x - 14\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Find } p, q: p \times q = -14,\; p + q = -5 \\ p = 2,\; q = -7 \\ x^2 - 5x - 14 = (x + 2)(x - 7)
\end{align*}\]
e) \(\displaystyle x^2 + 5x - 50\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Find } p, q: p \times q = -50,\; p + q = 5 \\ p = 10,\; q = -5 \\ x^2 + 5x - 50 = (x + 10)(x - 5)
\end{align*}\]
f) \(\displaystyle x^2 - x - 72\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Find } p, q: p \times q = -72,\; p + q = -1 \\ p = 8,\; q = -9 \\ x^2 - x - 72 = (x + 8)(x - 9)
\end{align*}\]
g) \(\displaystyle n^2 + 5n - 14\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Find } p, q: p \times q = -14,\; p + q = 5 \\ p = 7,\; q = -2 \\ n^2 + 5n - 14 = (n + 7)(n - 2)
\end{align*}\]
h) \(\displaystyle y^2 + 4y - 60\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Find } p, q: p \times q = -60,\; p + q = 4 \\ p = 10,\; q = -6 \\ y^2 + 4y - 60 = (y + 10)(y - 6)
\end{align*}\]





Exercise 6.3 Factorise using the difference of two squares.


a) \(\displaystyle x^2 - 1\)


b) \(\displaystyle y^2 - 121\)


c) \(\displaystyle 4x^2 - 100\)




d) \(\displaystyle 4m^2 - 49\)


e) \(\displaystyle 9x^2 - 25\)


f) \(\displaystyle n^2 - 121\)




g) \(\displaystyle n^2 - 4\)


h) \(\displaystyle 4m^2 - 9\)








Tip🔍 View Solutions





a) \(\displaystyle x^2 - 1\)
\[\begin{align*}
x^2 - 1^2 = (x + 1)(x - 1)
\end{align*}\]
b) \(\displaystyle y^2 - 121\)
\[\begin{align*}
y^2 - 11^2 = (y + 11)(y - 11)
\end{align*}\]
c) \(\displaystyle 4x^2 - 100\)
\[\begin{align*}
(2x)^2 - (10)^2 = (2x + 10)(2x - 10)
\end{align*}\]
d) \(\displaystyle 4m^2 - 49\)
\[\begin{align*}
(2m)^2 - (7)^2 = (2m + 7)(2m - 7)
\end{align*}\]
e) \(\displaystyle 9x^2 - 25\)
\[\begin{align*}
(3x)^2 - (5)^2 = (3x + 5)(3x - 5)
\end{align*}\]
f) \(\displaystyle n^2 - 121\)
\[\begin{align*}
n^2 - 11^2 = (n + 11)(n - 11)
\end{align*}\]
g) \(\displaystyle n^2 - 4\)
\[\begin{align*}
n^2 - 2^2 = (n + 2)(n - 2)
\end{align*}\]
h) \(\displaystyle 4m^2 - 9\)
\[\begin{align*}
(2m)^2 - (3)^2 = (2m + 3)(2m - 3)
\end{align*}\]





Exercise 6.4 Recognise and factorise each perfect square.


a) \(\displaystyle y^2 - 2y + 1\)


b) \(\displaystyle n^2 + 18n + 81\)




c) \(\displaystyle y^2 + 18y + 81\)


d) \(\displaystyle x^2 + 12x + 36\)




e) \(\displaystyle m^2 - 20m + 100\)


f) \(\displaystyle n^2 + 2n + 1\)








Tip🔍 View Solutions





a) \(\displaystyle y^2 - 2y + 1\)
\[\begin{align*}
(y - 1)^2 \quad \text{[perfect square]}
\end{align*}\]
b) \(\displaystyle n^2 + 18n + 81\)
\[\begin{align*}
(n + 9)^2 \quad \text{[perfect square]}
\end{align*}\]
c) \(\displaystyle y^2 + 18y + 81\)
\[\begin{align*}
(y + 9)^2 \quad \text{[perfect square]}
\end{align*}\]
d) \(\displaystyle x^2 + 12x + 36\)
\[\begin{align*}
(x + 6)^2 \quad \text{[perfect square]}
\end{align*}\]
e) \(\displaystyle m^2 - 20m + 100\)
\[\begin{align*}
(m - 10)^2 \quad \text{[perfect square]}
\end{align*}\]
f) \(\displaystyle n^2 + 2n + 1\)
\[\begin{align*}
(n + 1)^2 \quad \text{[perfect square]}
\end{align*}\]





Exercise 6.5 Solve each quadratic equation.


a) \(\displaystyle x^2 - 1 = 0\)


b) \(\displaystyle x^2 - 7x + 6 = 0\)




c) \(\displaystyle y^2 + y - 42 = 0\)


d) \(\displaystyle y^2 + 3y - 28 = 0\)




e) \(\displaystyle y^2 + 3y - 10 = 0\)


f) \(\displaystyle n^2 + 5n + 4 = 0\)




g) \(\displaystyle m^2 + 14m + 48 = 0\)


h) \(\displaystyle y^2 - 6y + 5 = 0\)








Tip🔍 View Solutions





a) \(\displaystyle x^2 - 1 = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Find } p, q: p \times q = -1,\; p + q = 0 \\ p = 1,\; q = -1 \\ (x + 1)(x - 1) = 0 \\ x = -1 \text{ or } x = 1
\end{align*}\]
b) \(\displaystyle x^2 - 7x + 6 = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Find } p, q: p \times q = 6,\; p + q = -7 \\ p = -1,\; q = -6 \\ (x - 1)(x - 6) = 0 \\ x = 1 \text{ or } x = 6
\end{align*}\]
c) \(\displaystyle y^2 + y - 42 = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Find } p, q: p \times q = -42,\; p + q = 1 \\ p = 7,\; q = -6 \\ (y + 7)(y - 6) = 0 \\ y = -7 \text{ or } y = 6
\end{align*}\]
d) \(\displaystyle y^2 + 3y - 28 = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Find } p, q: p \times q = -28,\; p + q = 3 \\ p = -4,\; q = 7 \\ (y - 4)(y + 7) = 0 \\ y = 4 \text{ or } y = -7
\end{align*}\]
e) \(\displaystyle y^2 + 3y - 10 = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Find } p, q: p \times q = -10,\; p + q = 3 \\ p = 5,\; q = -2 \\ (y + 5)(y - 2) = 0 \\ y = -5 \text{ or } y = 2
\end{align*}\]
f) \(\displaystyle n^2 + 5n + 4 = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Find } p, q: p \times q = 4,\; p + q = 5 \\ p = 1,\; q = 4 \\ (n + 1)(n + 4) = 0 \\ n = -1 \text{ or } n = -4
\end{align*}\]
g) \(\displaystyle m^2 + 14m + 48 = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Find } p, q: p \times q = 48,\; p + q = 14 \\ p = 6,\; q = 8 \\ (m + 6)(m + 8) = 0 \\ m = -6 \text{ or } m = -8
\end{align*}\]
h) \(\displaystyle y^2 - 6y + 5 = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Find } p, q: p \times q = 5,\; p + q = -6 \\ p = -5,\; q = -1 \\ (y - 5)(y - 1) = 0 \\ y = 5 \text{ or } y = 1
\end{align*}\]





∑ × π ÷ √ ◆ ∞ ± ∫ ≠ Δ




Exercise 6.6 Factorise each expression.


a) \(\displaystyle y^2 - 3y + 2\)


b) \(\displaystyle y^2 - 7y + 10\)




c) \(\displaystyle n^2 - 9n + 14\)


d) \(\displaystyle y^2 - 14y + 48\)




e) \(\displaystyle y^2 - 12y + 36\)


f) \(\displaystyle y^2 - 7y + 10\)








Tip🔍 View Solutions





a) \(\displaystyle y^2 - 3y + 2\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Find } p, q: p \times q = 2,\; p + q = -3 \\ p = -2,\; q = -1 \\ y^2 - 3y + 2 = (y - 2)(y - 1)
\end{align*}\]
b) \(\displaystyle y^2 - 7y + 10\)
\[\begin{align*}

Exercise 6.12  

A rectangle has length \((x + 3)\) cm and width \((x + 2)\) cm. The area is 42 cm².
(i) Form a quadratic equation in \(x\).
(ii) Solve the equation, giving exact answers.
(iii) Find the dimensions of the rectangle.






Tip🔍 View Solution





\[\begin{align*}
\text{(i) } (x + 3)(x + 2) = 42 \\ x^2 + 5x + 6 = 42 \\ x^2 +5x - 36 = 0 \\ \text{(ii) } x = \dfrac{-(5) \pm \sqrt{(5)^2 - 4(1)(-36)}}{2} \\ x = \dfrac{-5 \pm 13}{2} \\ x = 4 \text{ or } x = -9 \\ \text{(iii) Taking positive root: } x = 4 \\ \text{Length } = 4 + 3 = 7 \text{ cm, }\text{ Width } = 4 + 2 = 6 \text{ cm}
\end{align*}\]
Answer: \(x = 4\)

Exercise 6.24 Form a quadratic equation with roots \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\). Give your answer in the form \(y^2 + py + q = 0\).


a) \(\displaystyle \alpha = -1,\; \beta = -2\)




b) \(\displaystyle \alpha = -2,\; \beta = 5\)




c) \(\displaystyle \alpha = 2,\; \beta = 3\)




d) \(\displaystyle \alpha = 1,\; \beta = -4\)








Tip🔍 View Solutions





a) \(\displaystyle \alpha = -1,\; \beta = -2\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Sum of roots: } \alpha + \beta = -1 + (-2) = -3 \\ \text{Product of roots: } \alpha\beta = (-1) \times (-2) = 2 \\ \text{Equation: } y^2 - (\alpha+\beta)y + \alpha\beta = 0 \\ y^2 + 3y + 2 = 0
\end{align*}\]
b) \(\displaystyle \alpha = -2,\; \beta = 5\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Sum of roots: } \alpha + \beta = -2 + (5) = 3 \\ \text{Product of roots: } \alpha\beta = (-2) \times (5) = -10 \\ \text{Equation: } y^2 - (\alpha+\beta)y + \alpha\beta = 0 \\ y^2 - 3y - 10 = 0
\end{align*}\]
c) \(\displaystyle \alpha = 2,\; \beta = 3\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Sum of roots: } \alpha + \beta = 2 + (3) = 5 \\ \text{Product of roots: } \alpha\beta = (2) \times (3) = 6 \\ \text{Equation: } x^2 - (\alpha+\beta)x + \alpha\beta = 0 \\ x^2 - 5x + 6 = 0
\end{align*}\]
d) \(\displaystyle \alpha = 1,\; \beta = -4\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Sum of roots: } \alpha + \beta = 1 + (-4) = -3 \\ \text{Product of roots: } \alpha\beta = (1) \times (-4) = -4 \\ \text{Equation: } m^2 - (\alpha+\beta)m + \alpha\beta = 0 \\ m^2 + 3m - 4 = 0
\end{align*}\]

Exercise 6.29 Solve by completing the square.


a) \(\displaystyle n^2 + 2n - 2 = 0\)




b) \(\displaystyle n^2 + 8n + 7 = 0\)




c) \(\displaystyle n^2 + 2n - 4 = 0\)




d) \(\displaystyle n^2 - 4n - 7 = 0\)








Tip🔍 View Solutions





a) \(\displaystyle n^2 + 2n - 2 = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
n^2 + 2n - 2 = 0 \\ (n + 1)^2 - 3 = 0 \\ (n + 1)^2 = 3 \\ n + 1 = \pm \sqrt{3} \\ n = -1 + \sqrt{3} \text{ or } n = -1 - \sqrt{3}
\end{align*}\]
b) \(\displaystyle n^2 + 8n + 7 = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
n^2 + 8n + 7 = 0 \\ (n + 4)^2 - 9 = 0 \\ (n + 4)^2 = 9 \\ n + 4 = \pm 3 \\ n = -1 \text{ or } n = -7
\end{align*}\]
c) \(\displaystyle n^2 + 2n - 4 = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
n^2 + 2n - 4 = 0 \\ (n + 1)^2 - 5 = 0 \\ (n + 1)^2 = 5 \\ n + 1 = \pm \sqrt{5} \\ n = -1 + \sqrt{5} \text{ or } n = -1 - \sqrt{5}
\end{align*}\]
d) \(\displaystyle n^2 - 4n - 7 = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
n^2 - 4n - 7 = 0 \\ (n - 2)^2 - 11 = 0 \\ (n - 2)^2 = 11 \\ n - 2 = \pm \sqrt{11} \\ n = 2 + \sqrt{11} \text{ or } n = 2 - \sqrt{11}
\end{align*}\]





Exercise 6.30 Solve by completing the square.


a) \(\displaystyle 2y^2 - 4y = 0\)




b) \(\displaystyle 2x^2 + x - 1 = 0\)




c) \(\displaystyle 2m^2 - 5m - 1 = 0\)








Tip🔍 View Solutions





a) \(\displaystyle 2y^2 - 4y = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Divide by } 2: y^2 -2y +0 = 0 \\ \text{Complete the square:} \\ \left(y - 1\right)^2 = 1 \\ y - 1 = \pm 1 \\ y = 2 \text{ or } y = 0
\end{align*}\]
b) \(\displaystyle 2x^2 + x - 1 = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Divide by } 2: x^2 +\dfrac{1}{2}x -\dfrac{1}{2} = 0 \\ \text{Complete the square:} \\ \left(x + \dfrac{1}{4}\right)^2 = \dfrac{9}{16} \\ x + \dfrac{1}{4} = \pm \dfrac{3}{4} \\ x = \dfrac{1}{2} \text{ or } x = -1
\end{align*}\]
c) \(\displaystyle 2m^2 - 5m - 1 = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Divide by } 2: m^2 -\dfrac{5}{2}m -\dfrac{1}{2} = 0 \\ \text{Complete the square:} \\ \left(m - \dfrac{5}{4}\right)^2 = \dfrac{33}{16} \\ m - \dfrac{5}{4} = \pm \dfrac{\sqrt{33}}{4} \\ m = \dfrac{5}{4} + \dfrac{\sqrt{33}}{4} \text{ or } m = \dfrac{5}{4} - \dfrac{\sqrt{33}}{4}
\end{align*}\]





Exercise 6.31  

A ball is thrown upward with initial velocity 15 m/s. Its height (m) after \(t\) seconds is \(h = -5t^2 + 15t + 15\).
(i) What does the constant 15 represent in this context?
(ii) Find when the ball hits the ground. Give exact answers in surd form.
(iii) Find the time to 2 d.p. and interpret your answer.






Tip🔍 View Solution





\[\begin{align*}
\text{(i) The constant term 15 is the initial height (height at } t=0\text{).} \\ \text{(ii) Set } h=0: \\ -5t^2 + 15t + 15 = 0 \\ 5t^2 - 15t - 15 = 0 \\ t = \dfrac{-(-15) \pm \sqrt{(-15)^2 - 4(5)(-15)}}{10} \\ t = \dfrac{15 \pm \sqrt{525}}{10} \\ t = \dfrac{15 \pm 5\sqrt{21}}{10} \\ t = \dfrac{3 \pm \sqrt{21}}{2} \\ \text{(iii) Taking positive root: } t \approx 3.79 \text{ s} \\ \text{This is the time when the ball hits the ground.}
\end{align*}\]
Answer: \(t \approx 3.79 \text{ s (time to hit ground)}\)





Exercise 6.32

(iii) Find the time to 2 d.p. and interpret your answer.






Tip🔍 View Solution





\[\begin{align*}
\text{(i) The constant term 5 is the initial height (height at } t=0\text{).} \\ \text{(ii) Set } h=0: \\ -5t^2 + 15t + 5 = 0 \\ 5t^2 - 15t - 5 = 0 \\ t = \dfrac{-(-15) \pm \sqrt{(-15)^2 - 4(5)(-5)}}{10} \\ t = \dfrac{15 \pm \sqrt{325}}{10} \\ t = \dfrac{15 \pm 5\sqrt{13}}{10} \\ t = \dfrac{3 \pm \sqrt{13}}{2} \\ \text{(iii) Taking positive root: } t \approx 3.30 \text{ s} \\ \text{This is the time when the ball hits the ground.}
\end{align*}\]
Answer: \(t \approx 3.30 \text{ s (time to hit ground)}\)





Exercise 6.33  

A right-angled triangle has legs \(x\) cm and \((x + 2)\) cm. The hypotenuse is 10 cm.
(i) Use Pythagoras’ theorem to form a quadratic equation.
(ii) Solve the equation exactly.
(iii) Find the lengths of the two legs.






Tip🔍 View Solution





\[\begin{align*}
\text{(i) Pythagoras: } x^2 + (x+2)^2 = 10^2 \\ x^2 + x^2 + 4x + 4 = 100 \\ 2x^2 + 4x - 96 = 0 \\ \text{(ii) } x = \dfrac{-(4) \pm \sqrt{(4)^2 - 4(2)(-96)}}{4} \\ x = \dfrac{-4 \pm 28}{4} \\ x = 6 \text{ or } x = -8 \\ \text{(iii) } x = 6 \text{ cm}
\end{align*}\]
Answer: \(x = 6 \text{ cm, legs: } 6 \text{ and } 8 \text{ cm}\)

Tip🔍 View Solution





\[\begin{align*}

\end{align*}\]
Answer: \(x = 12 \text{ cm, legs: } 12 \text{ and } 16 \text{ cm}\)





Exercise 6.35 Find the value(s) of \(k\) for which the equation has real roots.


a) \(\displaystyle n^2 - 6n + k = 0\)




b) \(\displaystyle m^2 + km + 9 = 0\)




c) \(\displaystyle x^2 + 4x + k = 0\)




d) \(\displaystyle y^2 + 6y + k = 0\)








Tip🔍 View Solutions





a) \(\displaystyle n^2 - 6n + k = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Given: } n^2 - 6n + k = 0 \\ \text{For real roots: } \Delta \geq 0 \\ (-6)^2 - 4k \geq 0 \\ 36 \geq 4k \\ k \leq 9
\end{align*}\]
b) \(\displaystyle m^2 + km + 9 = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Given: } m^2 + km + 9 = 0 \\ \text{For real roots: } \Delta \geq 0 \\ k^2 - 4(9) \geq 0 \\ k^2 \geq 36 \\ k \leq -6 \text{ or } k \geq 6
\end{align*}\]
c) \(\displaystyle x^2 + 4x + k = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Given: } x^2 + 4x + k = 0 \\ \text{For real roots: } \Delta \geq 0 \\ (4)^2 - 4k \geq 0 \\ 16 \geq 4k \\ k \leq 4
\end{align*}\]
d) \(\displaystyle y^2 + 6y + k = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Given: } y^2 + 6y + k = 0 \\ \text{For real roots: } \Delta \geq 0 \\ (6)^2 - 4k \geq 0 \\ 36 \geq 4k \\ k \leq 9
\end{align*}\]





Exercise 6.36  

Two consecutive positive odd integers: the product of the larger and twice the smaller is 30.
(i) Form a quadratic equation in \(n\).
(ii) Solve the equation exactly.
(iii) Find the two integers.






Tip🔍 View Solution





\[\begin{align*}
text{(i) } (n+2)(2n) = 30 \\ 2n^2 + 4n - 30 = 0 \\ text{(ii) } n = \dfrac{-(4) \pm \sqrt{(4)^2 - 4(2)(-30)}}{4} \\ n = \dfrac{-4 \pm 16}{4} \\ n = 3 \text{ or } n = -5 \\ text{Since integers are positive odd, } n = 3 \\ text{Integers: } 3 \\ text{ and } 5
\end{align*}\]
Answer: \(Integers: 3 and 5\)

Exercise 6.39 The equations below have roots \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\). Use Vieta’s formulas to find the given expression.


a) \(\displaystyle 2m^2 + 6m - 5 = 0\)




b) \(\displaystyle x^2 + 6x + 6 = 0\)




c) \(\displaystyle x^2 + 2x - 3 = 0\)




d) \(\displaystyle y^2 + y - 2 = 0\)








Tip🔍 View Solutions





a) \(\displaystyle 2m^2 + 6m - 5 = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{From } 2m^2 + 6m - 5 = 0: \\ \alpha + \beta = \dfrac{-b}{a} = \dfrac{-6}{2} = -3 \\ \alpha\beta = \dfrac{c}{a} = \dfrac{-5}{2} = -\dfrac{5}{2} \\ \dfrac{1}{\alpha} + \dfrac{1}{\beta} = \dfrac{\alpha+\beta}{\alpha\beta} = \dfrac{-3}{-\dfrac{5}{2}} = \dfrac{6}{5}
\end{align*}\]
b) \(\displaystyle x^2 + 6x + 6 = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{From } x^2 + 6x + 6 = 0: \\ \alpha + \beta = \dfrac{-b}{a} = \dfrac{-6}{1} = -6 \\ \alpha\beta = \dfrac{c}{a} = \dfrac{6}{1} = 6 \\ \dfrac{1}{\alpha} + \dfrac{1}{\beta} = \dfrac{\alpha+\beta}{\alpha\beta} = \dfrac{-6}{6} = -1
\end{align*}\]
c) \(\displaystyle x^2 + 2x - 3 = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{From } x^2 + 2x - 3 = 0: \\ \alpha + \beta = \dfrac{-b}{a} = \dfrac{-2}{1} = -2 \\ \alpha\beta = \dfrac{c}{a} = \dfrac{-3}{1} = -3 \\ (\alpha - \beta)^2 = (\alpha+\beta)^2 - 4\alpha\beta = (-2)^2 - 4(-3) = 16
\end{align*}\]
d) \(\displaystyle y^2 + y - 2 = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{From } y^2 + y - 2 = 0: \\ \alpha + \beta = \dfrac{-b}{a} = \dfrac{-1}{1} = -1 \\ \alpha\beta = \dfrac{c}{a} = \dfrac{-2}{1} = -2 \\ \alpha^2 + \beta^2 = (\alpha+\beta)^2 - 2\alpha\beta = (-1)^2 - 2(-2) = 5
\end{align*}\]





Exercise 6.40

Shown is a right-angled triangle. All sides are in cm.
(a) Show that \(x^2 - 5x - 14 = 0\).
(b) Find \(x\).
(c) Find the area of the triangle.






Tip🔍 View Solution





\[\begin{align*}
\text{(a) Pythagoras: } (x + 5)^2 + (x - 2)^2 = (2x - 1)^2 \\ x^2 -5x -14 = 0 \\ x^2 - 5x - 14 = 0 \\ \text{(b) } x = \dfrac{-(-5) \pm \sqrt{(-5)^2 - 4(1)(-14)}}{2} \\ x = \dfrac{5 \pm 9}{2} \\ x = 7 \text{ or } x = -2 \\ \text{Taking positive root: } x = 7 \\ \text{(c) Legs: } 12 \text{ cm and } 5 \text{ cm} \\ \text{Area } = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 12 \times 5 = 30 \text{ cm}^2
\end{align*}\]
Answer: \(x = 7,\; \text{Area} = 30 \text{ cm}^2\)

Exercise 6.48 Solve. State any values of \(x\) that must be excluded.


a) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{4}{x} + \dfrac{4}{x - 3} = 2\)




b) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{5}{x} + \dfrac{10}{x - 6} = 3\)




c) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{6}{x} + \dfrac{12}{x - 5} = 3\)




d) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{6}{x} + \dfrac{12}{x - 5} = 3\)








Tip🔍 View Solutions





a) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{4}{x} + \dfrac{4}{x - 3} = 2\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Multiply through by } x(x - 3): \\ 4(x - 3) + 4x = 2x(x - 3) \\ 2x^2 - 14x + 12 = 0 \\ (x - 6)(x - 1) = 0 \\ x = 6 \text{ or } x = 1
\end{align*}\]
b) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{5}{x} + \dfrac{10}{x - 6} = 3\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Multiply through by } x(x - 6): \\ 5(x - 6) + 10x = 3x(x - 6) \\ 3x^2 - 33x + 30 = 0 \\ (x - 10)(x - 1) = 0 \\ x = 10 \text{ or } x = 1
\end{align*}\]
c) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{6}{x} + \dfrac{12}{x - 5} = 3\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Multiply through by } x(x - 5): \\ 6(x - 5) + 12x = 3x(x - 5) \\ 3x^2 - 33x + 30 = 0 \\ (x - 10)(x - 1) = 0 \\ x = 10 \text{ or } x = 1
\end{align*}\]
d) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{6}{x} + \dfrac{12}{x - 5} = 3\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Multiply through by } x(x - 5): \\ 6(x - 5) + 12x = 3x(x - 5) \\ 3x^2 - 33x + 30 = 0 \\ (x - 10)(x - 1) = 0 \\ x = 10 \text{ or } x = 1
\end{align*}\]





Exercise 6.49 Solve. State any values of \(x\) that must be excluded.


a) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{-4}{x + 5} + \dfrac{2}{x - 1} = -2\)




b) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{-3}{x + 1} - \dfrac{7}{x - 3} = 2\)




c) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{6}{x + 1} - \dfrac{1}{x - 1} = 1\)




d) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{8}{x + 4} + \dfrac{8}{x - 2} = 2\)








Tip🔍 View Solutions





a) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{-4}{x + 5} + \dfrac{2}{x - 1} = -2\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Multiply by } (x + 5)(x - 1): \\ -4(x - 1) + 2(x + 5) = -2(x + 5)(x - 1) \\ -2x^2 - 6x - 4 = 0 \\ (x + 2)(x + 1) = 0 \\ x = -2 \text{ or } x = -1
\end{align*}\]
b) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{-3}{x + 1} - \dfrac{7}{x - 3} = 2\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Multiply by } (x + 1)(x - 3): \\ -3(x - 3) - 7(x + 1) = 2(x + 1)(x - 3) \\ 2x^2 + 6x - 8 = 0 \\ (x + 4)(x -1) = 0 \\ x = -4 \text{ or } x = 1
\end{align*}\]
c) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{6}{x + 1} - \dfrac{1}{x - 1} = 1\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Multiply by } (x + 1)(x - 1): \\ 6(x - 1) - 1(x + 1) = 1(x + 1)(x - 1) \\ x^2 - 5x + 6 = 0 \\ (x -2)(x -3) = 0 \\ x = 2 \text{ or } x = 3
\end{align*}\]
d) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{8}{x + 4} + \dfrac{8}{x - 2} = 2\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Multiply by } (x + 4)(x - 2): \\ 8(x - 2) + 8(x + 4) = 2(x + 4)(x - 2) \\ 2x^2 - 12x - 32 = 0 \\ (x -8)(x + 2) = 0 \\ x = 8 \text{ or } x = -2
\end{align*}\]





Exercise 6.50 Solve using the substitution \(u = x^2\).


a) \(\displaystyle x^4 -10x^2 + 16 = 0\)




b) \(\displaystyle x^4 -6x^2 + 5 = 0\)




c) \(\displaystyle x^4 -5x^2 + 4 = 0\)




d) \(\displaystyle x^4 -5x^2 + 4 = 0\)








Tip🔍 View Solutions





a) \(\displaystyle x^4 -10x^2 + 16 = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Let } u = x^2: \quad u^2 -10u + 16 = 0 \\ (u -2)(u -8) = 0 \\ u = 2 \text{ or } u = 8 \\ x^2 = 2 \Rightarrow x = \pm\sqrt{2} \\ x^2 = 8 \Rightarrow x = \pm\sqrt{8}
\end{align*}\]
b) \(\displaystyle x^4 -6x^2 + 5 = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Let } u = x^2: \quad u^2 -6u + 5 = 0 \\ (u -1)(u -5) = 0 \\ u = 1 \text{ or } u = 5 \\ x^2 = 1 \Rightarrow x = \pm1 \\ x^2 = 5 \Rightarrow x = \pm\sqrt{5}
\end{align*}\]
c) \(\displaystyle x^4 -5x^2 + 4 = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Let } u = x^2: \quad u^2 -5u + 4 = 0 \\ (u -4)(u -1) = 0 \\ u = 4 \text{ or } u = 1 \\ x^2 = 4 \Rightarrow x = \pm 2 \\ x^2 = 1 \Rightarrow x = \pm 1
\end{align*}\]
d) \(\displaystyle x^4 -5x^2 + 4 = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Let } u = x^2: \quad u^2 -5u + 4 = 0 \\ (u -4)(u -1) = 0 \\ u = 4 \text{ or } u = 1 \\ x^2 = 4 \Rightarrow x = \pm 2 \\ x^2 = 1 \Rightarrow x = \pm 1
\end{align*}\]





Exercise 6.51 Solve using an appropriate substitution.


a) \(\displaystyle (x + 5)^2 + 8(x + 5) + 12 = 0\)




b) \(\displaystyle (x + 2)^2 - 5(x + 2) - 6 = 0\)




c) \(\displaystyle (x + 1)^2 + 7(x + 1) + 10 = 0\)




d) \(\displaystyle (x + 4)^2 + 5(x + 4) + 4 = 0\)








Tip🔍 View Solutions





a) \(\displaystyle (x + 5)^2 + 8(x + 5) + 12 = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Let } u = (x + 5): \quad u^2 + 8u + 12 = 0 \\ (u + 2)(u + 6) = 0 \\ u = -2 \Rightarrow x + 5 = -2 \Rightarrow x = -7 \\ u = -6 \Rightarrow x + 5 = -6 \Rightarrow x = -11
\end{align*}\]
b) \(\displaystyle (x + 2)^2 - 5(x + 2) - 6 = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Let } u = (x + 2): \quad u^2 - 5u - 6 = 0 \\ (u + 1)(u -6) = 0 \\ u = -1 \Rightarrow x + 2 = -1 \Rightarrow x = -3 \\ u = 6 \Rightarrow x + 2 = 6 \Rightarrow x = 4
\end{align*}\]
c) \(\displaystyle (x + 1)^2 + 7(x + 1) + 10 = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Let } u = (x + 1): \quad u^2 + 7u + 10 = 0 \\ (u + 2)(u + 5) = 0 \\ u = -2 \Rightarrow x + 1 = -2 \Rightarrow x = -3 \\ u = -5 \Rightarrow x + 1 = -5 \Rightarrow x = -6
\end{align*}\]
d) \(\displaystyle (x + 4)^2 + 5(x + 4) + 4 = 0\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Let } u = (x + 4): \quad u^2 + 5u + 4 = 0 \\ (u + 1)(u + 4) = 0 \\ u = -1 \Rightarrow x + 4 = -1 \Rightarrow x = -5 \\ u = -4 \Rightarrow x + 4 = -4 \Rightarrow x = -8
\end{align*}\]





Exercise 6.52 Solve. Check for extraneous roots.


a) \(\displaystyle \sqrt{x + 74} = x + 2\)




b) \(\displaystyle \sqrt{x + 29} = x - 1\)




c) \(\displaystyle \sqrt{x + 41} = x - 1\)




d) \(\displaystyle \sqrt{x + 8} = x + 2\)








Tip🔍 View Solutions





a) \(\displaystyle \sqrt{x + 74} = x + 2\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Square both sides: } x + 74 = (x + 2)^2 \\ x^2 + 3x - 70 = 0 \\ (x -7)(x + 10) = 0 \\ x = 7 \text{ or } x = -10 \\ \text{Check both in original equation:} \\ \text{Check } x=-10: 8 = 8 \neq -8 \text{ (extraneous)} \\ \text{Valid: } x = 7
\end{align*}\]
b) \(\displaystyle \sqrt{x + 29} = x - 1\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Square both sides: } x + 29 = (x - 1)^2 \\ x^2 - 3x - 28 = 0 \\ (x -7)(x + 4) = 0 \\ x = 7 \text{ or } x = -4 \\ \text{Check both in original equation:} \\ \text{Check } x=-4: 5 = 5 \neq -5 \text{ (extraneous)} \\ \text{Valid: } x = 7
\end{align*}\]
c) \(\displaystyle \sqrt{x + 41} = x - 1\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Square both sides: } x + 41 = (x - 1)^2 \\ x^2 - 3x - 40 = 0 \\ (x -8)(x + 5) = 0 \\ x = 8 \text{ or } x = -5 \\ \text{Check both in original equation:} \\ \text{Check } x=-5: 6 = 6 \neq -6 \text{ (extraneous)} \\ \text{Valid: } x = 8
\end{align*}\]
d) \(\displaystyle \sqrt{x + 8} = x + 2\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Square both sides: } x + 8 = (x + 2)^2 \\ x^2 + 3x - 4 = 0 \\ (x -1)(x + 4) = 0 \\ x = 1 \text{ or } x = -4 \\ \text{Check both in original equation:} \\ \text{Check } x=-4: 2 = 2 \neq -2 \text{ (extraneous)} \\ \text{Valid: } x = 1
\end{align*}\]





Exercise 6.53 Solve. Check for extraneous roots.


a) \(\displaystyle \sqrt{x + 3} - \sqrt{7 - x} = 2\)




b) \(\displaystyle \sqrt{x + 5} - \sqrt{8 - x} = 1\)




c) \(\displaystyle \sqrt{x + 1} - \sqrt{4 - x} = 1\)








Tip🔍 View Solutions





a) \(\displaystyle \sqrt{x + 3} - \sqrt{7 - x} = 2\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Isolate: } \sqrt{x + 3} = 2 + \sqrt{7-x} \\ \text{Square: } x + 3 = 2^2 + 2(2)\sqrt{7-x} + (7-x) \\ 2(2)\sqrt{7-x} = 2x - 8 \\ \text{Square again: } 4(4)(7-x) = (2x -8)^2 \\ 4x^2 -16x -48 = 0 \\ x = 6 \\ \text{(check both roots in original)}
\end{align*}\]
b) \(\displaystyle \sqrt{x + 5} - \sqrt{8 - x} = 1\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Isolate: } \sqrt{x + 5} = 1 + \sqrt{8-x} \\ \text{Square: } x + 5 = 1^2 + 2(1)\sqrt{8-x} + (8-x) \\ 2(1)\sqrt{8-x} = 2x - 4 \\ \text{Square again: } 4(1)(8-x) = (2x -4)^2 \\ 4x^2 -12x -16 = 0 \\ x = 4 \\ \text{(check both roots in original)}
\end{align*}\]
c) \(\displaystyle \sqrt{x + 1} - \sqrt{4 - x} = 1\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Isolate: } \sqrt{x + 1} = 1 + \sqrt{4-x} \\ \text{Square: } x + 1 = 1^2 + 2(1)\sqrt{4-x} + (4-x) \\ 2(1)\sqrt{4-x} = 2x - 4 \\ \text{Square again: } 4(1)(4-x) = (2x -4)^2 \\ 4x^2 -12x +0 = 0 \\ x = 3 \\ \text{(check both roots in original)}
\end{align*}\]





Exercise 6.54 Solve. (Hint: use the index law \((a^m)^n = a^{mn}\), equate exponents, then solve the resulting quadratic.)


a) \(\displaystyle \left(2^{x - 2}\right)^{x - 2} = 16\)




b) \(\displaystyle \left(2^{x}\right)^{x - 4} = 32\)




c) \(\displaystyle \left(2^{x - 3}\right)^{x - 3} = 16\)




d) \(\displaystyle \left(2^{x - 4}\right)^{x} = 32\)








Tip🔍 View Solutions





a) \(\displaystyle \left(2^{x - 2}\right)^{x - 2} = 16\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Use index law: } (a^m)^n = a^{mn} \\ 2^{(x - 2)(x - 2)} = 2^{4} \\ \text{Equate exponents: } (x - 2)(x - 2) = 4 \\ x^2 -4x + 4 = 4 \\ x^2 -4x  = 0 \\ x = \dfrac{-(-4) \pm \sqrt{(-4)^2 - 4(1)(0)}}{2} \\ x = \dfrac{4 \pm 4}{2} \\ x = 4 \text{ or } x = 0
\end{align*}\]
b) \(\displaystyle \left(2^{x}\right)^{x - 4} = 32\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Use index law: } (a^m)^n = a^{mn} \\ 2^{(x)(x - 4)} = 2^{5} \\ \text{Equate exponents: } (x)(x - 4) = 5 \\ x^2 -4x  = 5 \\ x^2 -4x -5 = 0 \\ x = \dfrac{-(-4) \pm \sqrt{(-4)^2 - 4(1)(-5)}}{2} \\ x = \dfrac{4 \pm 6}{2} \\ x = 5 \text{ or } x = -1
\end{align*}\]
c) \(\displaystyle \left(2^{x - 3}\right)^{x - 3} = 16\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Use index law: } (a^m)^n = a^{mn} \\ 2^{(x - 3)(x - 3)} = 2^{4} \\ \text{Equate exponents: } (x - 3)(x - 3) = 4 \\ x^2 -6x + 9 = 4 \\ x^2 -6x + 5 = 0 \\ x = \dfrac{-(-6) \pm \sqrt{(-6)^2 - 4(1)(5)}}{2} \\ x = \dfrac{6 \pm 4}{2} \\ x = 5 \text{ or } x = 1
\end{align*}\]
d) \(\displaystyle \left(2^{x - 4}\right)^{x} = 32\)
\[\begin{align*}
\text{Use index law: } (a^m)^n = a^{mn} \\ 2^{(x - 4)(x)} = 2^{5} \\ \text{Equate exponents: } (x - 4)(x) = 5 \\ x^2 -4x  = 5 \\ x^2 -4x -5 = 0 \\ x = \dfrac{-(-4) \pm \sqrt{(-4)^2 - 4(1)(-5)}}{2} \\ x = \dfrac{4 \pm 6}{2} \\ x = 5 \text{ or } x = -1
\end{align*}\]

title: 7  Unit 7: Linear & Quadratic Functions
text:
7.2 🗂️ Formula Card

Linear & Quadratic Functions — Key Facts

Relations & Functions

💡 Key Test A relation is a function if every input (\(x\)-value) maps to exactly one output (\(y\)-value). Use the vertical line test on a graph.


Function notation: \(f(x) = mx + c\) means “the output when the input is \(x\)”
Domain: the set of all valid inputs (\(x\)-values)
Range: the set of all valid outputs (\(y\)-values)
Examples: \[f(x) = 2x + 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f(3) = 2(3)+1 = 7\]



Linear Functions

📝 Slope Formula \[m = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}\]


Slope-intercept form: \(y = mx + c\), where \(m\) = slope, \(c\) = \(y\)-intercept
Parallel lines share the same slope: \(m_1 = m_2\)
Perpendicular lines: slopes are negative reciprocals: \(m_1 \times m_2 = -1\)
Examples: \[\text{Through }(2,5)\text{ with slope }3: \quad y - 5 = 3(x-2) \quad \Rightarrow \quad y = 3x - 1\] \[\text{Perpendicular to }y=2x+1: \quad m_\perp = -\tfrac{1}{2}\]



Quadratic Functions — Three Forms

⚠️ Conversion Tip Each form reveals different information. Convert using expansion or completing the square.





Form
Equation
Reveals




Standard
\(f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c\)
\(y\)-intercept \((0, c)\)


Vertex
\(f(x) = a(x-h)^2 + k\)
Vertex \((h, k)\)


Factored
\(f(x) = a(x-r_1)(x-r_2)\)
Roots \(x = r_1, r_2\)



Vertex formula (from standard form): \[x_{\text{vertex}} = \frac{-b}{2a}\]
Axis of symmetry: \(x = \dfrac{-b}{2a} = h\)



Converting Between Forms

Vertex → Standard (expand): \[2(x-3)^2 + 1 = 2(x^2-6x+9)+1 = 2x^2 - 12x + 19\]
Vertex → Factored (find roots first): \[\textstyle(x-3)^2 - 4 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 3\pm2 \Rightarrow f(x)=(x-1)(x-5)\]
Standard → Vertex (complete the square): \[x^2 - 6x + 5 = (x-3)^2 - 9 + 5 = (x-3)^2 - 4\]
Factored → Standard (expand): \[(x-1)(x-5) = x^2 - 6x + 5\]



Domain & Range

🎯 Quick Rule Every quadratic has domain \(x \in \mathbb{R}\). The range depends on whether the parabola opens up or down.





Function type
Domain
Range




Linear \(f(x) = mx+c\)
\(x \in \mathbb{R}\)
\(y \in \mathbb{R}\)


Quadratic, \(a > 0\) (opens up)
\(x \in \mathbb{R}\)
\(y \geq k\)


Quadratic, \(a < 0\) (opens down)
\(x \in \mathbb{R}\)
\(y \leq k\)

text:
7.3 📝 Practice Problems

🟢 Easy🟡 Medium🔴 Challenging



Exercise 7.1 Analyse the arrow diagram.
The arrow diagram shows a relation.

   Domain Range 1 6 3 4 2 6    

(a) State the domain and range of this relation.
(b) Is this relation a function? Justify your answer.






Tip🔍 View Solution





\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Check: does each domain element have exactly one arrow?} \\[4pt] \text{No — one input maps to more than one output.}
\end{aligned}
\]





Function definition Domain & range Arrow diagrams




Exercise 7.2 Analyse the relation.
Consider the relation: \(\{\, (2,\ -1), (3,\ 4), (-4,\ -1), (5,\ -3), (5,\ -2) \,\}\)
(a) State the domain and range.
(b) Is this a function? Explain.






Tip🔍 View Solution





\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Domain: } \{-4, 2, 3, 5\} \\[4pt] \text{Range: } \{-3, -2, -1, 4\} \\[4pt] \text{Function? No — one $x$-value maps to two outputs.}
\end{aligned}
\]





Function definition Domain & range Arrow diagrams




Exercise 7.3 Find the equation of the linear function shown in the table.


a) \(\displaystyle \begin{array}{c|cccc} x & -2 & -1 & 3 & 4 \\ \hline y & 3 & 0 & -12 & -15 \end{array}\)


b) \(\displaystyle \begin{array}{c|cccc} x & -3 & -2 & 0 & 3 \\ \hline y & -3 & -2 & 0 & 3 \end{array}\)


c) \(\displaystyle \begin{array}{c|cccc} x & -3 & 0 & 1 & 3 \\ \hline y & -9 & 0 & 3 & 9 \end{array}\)


d) \(\displaystyle \begin{array}{c|cccc} x & -3 & 0 & 2 & 3 \\ \hline y & -5 & 4 & 10 & 13 \end{array}\)








Tip🔍 View Solutions





a)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Slope: } m = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{-3}{1} = -3 \\[4pt] \text{Substitute } (-2, 3): 3 = -3(-2) + c \\[4pt] c = -3 \\[4pt] y = -3x - 3
\end{aligned}
\] b)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Slope: } m = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{1}{1} = 1 \\[4pt] \text{Substitute } (-3, -3): -3 = 1(-3) + c \\[4pt] c = 0 \\[4pt] y = x
\end{aligned}
\] c)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Slope: } m = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{9}{3} = 3 \\[4pt] \text{Substitute } (-3, -9): -9 = 3(-3) + c \\[4pt] c = 0 \\[4pt] y = 3x
\end{aligned}
\] d)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Slope: } m = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{9}{3} = 3 \\[4pt] \text{Substitute } (-3, -5): -5 = 3(-3) + c \\[4pt] c = 4 \\[4pt] y = 3x + 4
\end{aligned}
\]





Table of values Linear equations Representations




Exercise 7.4 Complete the table of values.


a) \(\displaystyle y = -2x + 2 \qquad \begin{array}{c|cccc} x & -1 & 0 & 1 & 4 \\ \hline y & \square & 2 & 0 & \square \end{array}\)


b) \(\displaystyle y = -x \qquad \begin{array}{c|cccc} x & -2 & -1 & 3 & 4 \\ \hline y & 2 & 1 & \square & \square \end{array}\)


c) \(\displaystyle y = 2x + 2 \qquad \begin{array}{c|cccc} x & -1 & 0 & 2 & 3 \\ \hline y & 0 & \square & 6 & \square \end{array}\)


d) \(\displaystyle y = -x - 4 \qquad \begin{array}{c|cccc} x & 0 & 1 & 3 & 4 \\ \hline y & -4 & \square & \square & -8 \end{array}\)








Tip🔍 View Solutions





a)
\[
\begin{aligned}
x = -1: \quad y = -2(-1) + 2 = 4 \\[4pt] x = 4: \quad y = -2(4) + 2 = -6
\end{aligned}
\] b)
\[
\begin{aligned}
x = 3: \quad y = -1(3) + 0 = -3 \\[4pt] x = 4: \quad y = -1(4) + 0 = -4
\end{aligned}
\] c)
\[
\begin{aligned}
x = 0: \quad y = 2(0) + 2 = 2 \\[4pt] x = 3: \quad y = 2(3) + 2 = 8
\end{aligned}
\] d)
\[
\begin{aligned}
x = 1: \quad y = -1(1) - 4 = -5 \\[4pt] x = 3: \quad y = -1(3) - 4 = -7
\end{aligned}
\]





Table of values Linear equations Representations




Exercise 7.5 State the domain and range of each relation.


a) \(\displaystyle \{\,(1,\ -1), (3,\ 2), (0,\ -2), (-1,\ 4), (4,\ 0)\,\}\)


b) \(\displaystyle \{\,(2,\ 2), (1,\ -1), (-2,\ 4), (3,\ -3), (-4,\ -3)\,\}\)


c) \(\displaystyle \{\,(2,\ -3), (3,\ 4), (0,\ 4), (-4,\ -3), (4,\ 0)\,\}\)


d) \(\displaystyle \{\,(1,\ -3), (0,\ 2), (-2,\ 3), (-4,\ 0), (4,\ -2)\,\}\)








Tip🔍 View Solutions





a)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Domain (all }x\text{-values): } \{-1, 0, 1, 3, 4\} \\[4pt] \text{Range (all }y\text{-values): } \{-2, -1, 0, 2, 4\}
\end{aligned}
\] b)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Domain (all }x\text{-values): } \{-4, -2, 1, 2, 3\} \\[4pt] \text{Range (all }y\text{-values): } \{-3, -1, 2, 4\}
\end{aligned}
\] c)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Domain (all }x\text{-values): } \{-4, 0, 2, 3, 4\} \\[4pt] \text{Range (all }y\text{-values): } \{-3, 0, 4\}
\end{aligned}
\] d)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Domain (all }x\text{-values): } \{-4, -2, 0, 1, 4\} \\[4pt] \text{Range (all }y\text{-values): } \{-3, -2, 0, 2, 3\}
\end{aligned}
\]





Domain Range Set notation




Exercise 7.6 State the domain and range of each function.


a) \(\displaystyle f(x) = 3x - 1\)


b) \(\displaystyle f(x) = 3x + 3\)


c) \(\displaystyle f(x) = 2x - 2\)


d) \(\displaystyle f(x) = -2x\)








Tip🔍 View Solutions





a)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Linear functions are defined for all real numbers.} \\[4pt] \text{Domain: } x \in \mathbb{R} \\[4pt] \text{Range: } y \in \mathbb{R}
\end{aligned}
\] b)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Linear functions are defined for all real numbers.} \\[4pt] \text{Domain: } x \in \mathbb{R} \\[4pt] \text{Range: } y \in \mathbb{R}
\end{aligned}
\] c)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Linear functions are defined for all real numbers.} \\[4pt] \text{Domain: } x \in \mathbb{R} \\[4pt] \text{Range: } y \in \mathbb{R}
\end{aligned}
\] d)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Linear functions are defined for all real numbers.} \\[4pt] \text{Domain: } x \in \mathbb{R} \\[4pt] \text{Range: } y \in \mathbb{R}
\end{aligned}
\]





Domain Range Set notation




Exercise 7.7 Identify which expression represents a linear function.


a) \(\displaystyle \text{A:}\; |t| + 1 \qquad \text{B:}\; \dfrac{1}{t} + 4 \qquad \text{C:}\; 2t^2 + t + 1 \qquad \text{D:}\; -2t + 3\)


b) \(\displaystyle \text{A:}\; \dfrac{1}{y} + 1 \qquad \text{B:}\; |y| + 5 \qquad \text{C:}\; -2y - 5 \qquad \text{D:}\; 2y^2 - 3y + 3\)


c) \(\displaystyle \text{A:}\; 2n - 3 \qquad \text{B:}\; 3n^2 - 2n + 4 \qquad \text{C:}\; \dfrac{1}{n} + 2 \qquad \text{D:}\; |n| + 3\)


d) \(\displaystyle \text{A:}\; 2m^2 - 2m - 1 \qquad \text{B:}\; |m| + 3 \qquad \text{C:}\; \dfrac{1}{m} + 1 \qquad \text{D:}\; m + 3\)








Tip🔍 View Solutions





a)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{A linear function has degree 1 (form } f(x) = mx + c\text{).} \\[4pt] \text{Answer: } D \text{ — } f(x) = -2t + 3 \text{ has degree 1. }\checkmark
\end{aligned}
\] b)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{A linear function has degree 1 (form } f(x) = mx + c\text{).} \\[4pt] \text{Answer: } C \text{ — } f(x) = -2y - 5 \text{ has degree 1. }\checkmark
\end{aligned}
\] c)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{A linear function has degree 1 (form } f(x) = mx + c\text{).} \\[4pt] \text{Answer: } A \text{ — } f(x) = 2n - 3 \text{ has degree 1. }\checkmark
\end{aligned}
\] d)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{A linear function has degree 1 (form } f(x) = mx + c\text{).} \\[4pt] \text{Answer: } D \text{ — } f(x) = m + 3 \text{ has degree 1. }\checkmark
\end{aligned}
\]





Linear functions Slope y-intercept




Exercise 7.8 Evaluate the function at each given value.


a) \(\displaystyle f(x) = 2x - 3 \quad \text{Find: } f(-3), f(4), f(2)\)


b) \(\displaystyle f(x) = 3x - 6 \quad \text{Find: } f(0), f(-3), f(3)\)


c) \(\displaystyle f(x) = -x - 5 \quad \text{Find: } f(0), f(-1), f(2)\)

\[

Tip🔍 View Solutions





a)
\[
